## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Calorific Value of Paddy Straw using Dulong's Equation

**Problem:** A representative paddy straw sample (variety *Uma*, from Kuttanad region) has the following ultimate analysis (dry, ash-free basis):

- Carbon (C) = 38.5 %
- Hydrogen (H) = 5.4 %
- Oxygen (O) = 35.1 %
- Sulphur (S) = 0.1 %
- Nitrogen (N) = 0.6 %
- Ash = 20.3 %

Compute its **HHV using Dulong's empirical formula** and compare with experimental bomb-calorimeter value of 14.8 MJ/kg.

**Step 1 — State the Dulong equation for HHV in kJ/kg**

$$
HHV = 338\,C + 1442\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 927\,S
$$

**Step 2 — Convert ultimate analysis to "as-fired" basis by removing ash contribution**

The elements C, H, O, S, N reported on a **dry, ash-free (daf)** basis must be scaled to a **dry basis (db)** using the ash fraction.

Let $f_{db} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Ash}}{100} = 1 - 0.203 = 0.797$.

Therefore, on dry basis:
- $C_{db} = 38.5 \times 0.797 = 30.69$ %
- $H_{db} = 5.4 \times 0.797 = 4.30$ %
- $O_{db} = 35.1 \times 0.797 = 27.97$ %
- $S_{db} = 0.1 \times 0.797 = 0.0797$ %

**Step 3 — Plug into Dulong's equation**

$$
HHV = (338)(30.69) + 1442\!\left(4.30 - \dfrac{27.97}{8}\right) + (927)(0.0797)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the hydrogen-correction term**

$$
\dfrac{O}{8} = \dfrac{27.97}{8} = 3.496
$$

$$
H - \dfrac{O}{8} = 4.30 - 3.496 = 0.804
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate each term individually**

$$
\text{Term 1} = 338 \times 30.69 = 10{,}373.22 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
\text{Term 2} = 1442 \times 0.804 = 1{,}159.37 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
\text{Term 3} = 927 \times 0.0797 = 73.88 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Sum the terms**

$$
HHV = 10{,}373.22 + 1{,}159.37 + 73.88 = 11{,}606.47 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to MJ/kg and report**

$$
\boxed{HHV_{theoretical} = 11.61 \text{ MJ/kg}}
$$

**Step 8 — Compare with experimental value and discuss deviation**

$$
\text{Deviation} = \dfrac{14.80 - 11.61}{14.80} \times 100 = 21.55 \text{ \%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's note:** The deviation is acceptable because Dulong's formula was empirically fit for **coals** (high fixed-carbon, low ash). Paddy straw's high silica ash catalyses side reactions and absorbs heat as sensible ash, lowering the *effective* experimental HHV if measured on a *dry* basis. Use the as-fired basis consistently and consider **Channiwala–Parikh correlation** for higher accuracy on biomass (predicted ≈ 15.0 MJ/kg, deviation ≈ 1.4 %).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass & Energy Balance for a Paddy Straw Briquetting Plant

**Problem:** A small-scale briquetting unit in Palakkad processes **1000 kg/day** of raw paddy straw (MC = 12 % wb) into cylindrical briquettes (8 cm dia × 6 cm length, density 700 kg/m³). The mechanical piston press consumes **45 kWh/tonne** of raw feed. Compute the **net energy output** of the briquettes and the **Energy Return on Investment (EROI)**.

**Step 1 — Determine the mass of dry matter entering the system**

$$
M_{dry} = M_{raw} \times (1 - MC_{wb}) = 1000 \times (1 - 0.12) = 880 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply mass balance on the press (assume 2 % dry-matter loss as dust)**

$$
M_{briquette} = 0.98 \times 880 = 862.4 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
M_{dust} = 17.6 \text{ kg/day (collected as boiler fuel)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the energy content of briquettes (HHV = 15.0 MJ/kg)**

$$
E_{out} = 862.4 \times 15.0 = 12{,}936 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the energy input to the press**

$$
E_{in} = 45 \text{ kWh/tonne} = 45 \times 3.6 = 162 \text{ MJ/tonne} = 162 \text{ MJ/day (per tonne)}
$$

For 1 tonne/day, $E_{in} = 162$ MJ/day.

**Step 5 — Compute EROI**

$$
EROI = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \dfrac{12{,}936}{162} = 79.85
$$

$$
\boxed{EROI \approx 79.9}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** An EROI of ~80 means the briquetting process releases **~80 units of combustion energy for every 1 unit of electrical/mechanical energy** invested. This is comparable to wind (EROI ≈ 18–25) and far exceeds photovoltaic (EROI ≈ 5–10). Paddy straw briquetting is therefore a **highly energy-positive** valorization route — a key take-home point for KTU 14-mark answers.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Silica Removal Efficiency by NaOH Treatment

**Problem:** A 100 g sample of paddy straw (raw SiO₂ = 14.0 % db) is treated with 6 % NaOH for 24 h at 30 °C. After washing and drying, the treated straw weighs 78 g and has SiO₂ = 4.2 % db. Compute the **silica removal efficiency** and the **mass loss**.

**Step 1 — Compute initial silica mass**

$$
m_{SiO_2,\,raw} = 100 \times 0.140 = 14.0 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute final silica mass**

$$
m_{SiO_2,\,treated} = 78 \times 0.042 = 3.276 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute silica removal efficiency**

$$
\eta_{SiO_2} = \dfrac{14.0 - 3.276}{14.0} \times 100 = 76.6 \text{ \%}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute overall mass loss (suggests hemicellulose + lignin + silica leaching)**

$$
\text{Mass loss} = \dfrac{100 - 78}{100} \times 100 = 22 \text{ \%}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{SiO_2} \approx 76.6 \text{ \%}, \quad \text{Total mass loss} = 22 \text{ \%}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanism:** NaOH dissolves silica via $\text{SiO}_2 + 2\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SiO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$, and cleaves the **ester cross-links** between lignin and hemicellulose. The 22 % mass loss is dominated by hemicellulose (sugars) and partially-degraded lignin — a *trade-off* the student should discuss in KTU answers: higher digestibility vs. lower mass recovery.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define paddy straw. List its three main chemical constituents and state why it is difficult to use as direct animal fodder.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Paddy straw is the ligno-cellulosic residue (culms, leaves, and awns) left after harvesting and threshing of rice (*Oryza sativa*).
Its three main chemical constituents are:
1. **Cellulose** (32–47 %) — structural carbohydrate, digestible
2. **Hemicellulose** (19–27 %) — branched polysaccharide, partially digestible
3. **Lignin** (5–24 %) — aromatic polymer, **indigestible** to ruminants

Additionally, the straw has a high **silica (SiO₂: 8–16 %)** content forming a protective cuticle. The lignin–silica seal prevents rumen microbes from accessing cellulose, giving paddy straw a low *in vivo* digestibility of only **35–45 %** compared to 65 % for green fodder. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
*State Dulong's formula for Higher Heating Value (HHV) of a biomass. A paddy straw sample has 36 % C, 5 % H, 38 % O and 0.2 % S (all on dry basis). Calculate HHV.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dulong's empirical formula:
$$
HHV = 338\,C + 1442\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 927\,S \;\; \text{(kJ/kg)}
$$
Substituting the values:
$$
HHV = (338)(36) + 1442\!\left(5 - \dfrac{38}{8}\right) + (927)(0.2)
$$
$$
= 12{,}168 + 1442(0.25) + 185.4 = 12{,}168 + 360.5 + 185.4
$$
$$
\boxed{HHV = 12{,}713.9 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 12.71 \text{ MJ/kg}}
$$
**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often write "paddy straw can be used as fodder" **without** explaining *why it needs processing* — always start with the *lignin–silica barrier* problem statement.
> 2. Numerical answers must show **all substitution steps**; skipping the conversion of ultimate analysis from *daf → dry basis* costs **2 marks**.
> 3. Flow diagrams in 14-mark answers must show **by-product streams** (black liquor, digestate, biochar) — omission = -1 mark.
> 4. Avoid "memory-dump" mode: KTU 2024 scheme expects **CO-mapped** answers — explicitly link the process to a Course Outcome (e.g., CO₂: "understand biomass conversion technologies").

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **physical/mechanical processing of paddy straw** for solid biofuel production. With a neat flow diagram, describe the unit operations of **chopping → grinding → drying → densification (pelletization)**. State the typical operating conditions (pressure, temperature, particle size) and discuss the role of natural lignin as a binder. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A paddy straw briquetting plant receives **5 tonnes/day** of raw straw (MC = 14 % wb). After sun-drying to MC = 10 % wb and briquetting at 120 °C and 150 MPa, the briquette density is 680 kg/m³ and HHV is 15.2 MJ/kg. The hammer mill consumes 38 kWh/tonne and the press consumes 60 kWh/tonne of *dried* feed.
  (i) Compute the mass of briquettes produced per day (assume 3 % dry-matter loss).
  (ii) Compute the total daily energy output of briquettes.
  (iii) Calculate the EROI of the plant.
  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Physical/Mechanical Processing (7 marks):**

- **Step 1 – Chopping (1 mark):** Field choppers (chaff cutters, forage harvesters) reduce 50–100 cm straws to **3–5 cm** pieces. This improves bulk handling, drying uniformity, and mill feeding.

- **Step 2 – Drying (1 mark):** Sun-drying on a cement drying floor for **2–3 days** (target MC 10–12 % wb) or rotary drum dryers at 80–120 °C for industrial scale. Lower MC improves calorific value, prevents mould, and enables the lignin-softening during densification.

- **Step 3 – Grinding (2 marks):** Hammer mills with 2–4 mm screens reduce particles to **<2 mm powder**. Energy consumption: 30–50 kWh/tonne. Ball mills or knife mills can further reduce to <0.5 mm for pellet-grade fuel. Smaller particles → larger surface area → better combustion.

- **Step 4 – Densification (Pelletization/Briquetting) (2 marks):** Ring-die or flat-die pellet mills compact the powder at **80–120 °C** and **100–200 MPa**. At these conditions, **natural lignin softens (Tg ≈ 100 °C)** and binds the particles. The pellets are 6–10 mm diameter with density 600–750 kg/m³, then cooled in a counterflow cooler.

- **Step 5 – Quality Control (1 mark):** Hardness test (≥ 50 N), MC check, dust content (< 1 %), and packaging in laminated PP bags.

**Flow Diagram (refer to Section 4.2 of these notes).**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Mass of briquettes (3 marks):**
- Mass of dry matter in raw straw:
$$
M_{dry,\,raw} = 5000 \times (1 - 0.14) = 4300 \text{ kg/day}
$$
- Mass of dry matter in dried straw (negligible DM loss during drying):
$$
M_{dry,\,dried} = 4300 \text{ kg db}
$$
- Mass of dried straw (wet basis at 10 % MC):
$$
M_{dried,\,wb} = \dfrac{4300}{1 - 0.10} = 4777.78 \text{ kg/day}
$$
- Mass of briquettes (after 3 % DM loss):
$$
M_{briquettes} = 4300 \times 0.97 = 4171 \text{ kg db}
$$
- Mass of briquettes on wet basis (assuming briquettes at 8 % MC):
$$
M_{briquettes,\,wb} = \dfrac{4171}{0.92} \approx 4533.7 \text{ kg/day}
$$
$$
\boxed{M_{briquettes} \approx 4534 \text{ kg/day}}
$$

**(ii) Total daily energy output (2 marks):**
$$
E_{out} = 4533.7 \times 15.2 = 68{,}912 \text{ MJ/day}
$$
Converting to kWh: $\dfrac{68{,}912}{3.6} = 19{,}142$ kWh/day.
$$
\boxed{E_{out} \approx 68{,}912 \text{ MJ/day} \approx 19{,}142 \text{ kWh/day}}
$$

**(iii) EROI (2 marks):**
- Mill consumption: 38 kWh/tonne of raw straw → $38 \times 5 = 190$ kWh/day.
- Press consumption: 60 kWh/tonne of *dried* feed → $60 \times 4.778 = 286.7$ kWh/day.
- Total input:
$$
E_{in} = (190 + 286.7) \times 3.6 = 1716 \text{ MJ/day}
$$
- EROI:
$$
EROI = \dfrac{68{,}912}{1716} = 40.16
$$
$$
\boxed{EROI \approx 40.2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** An EROI of 40 is excellent, confirming that mechanical densification of paddy straw is a *highly energy-positive* valorization route.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]

**(a)** Describe the **chemical pretreatment of paddy straw** for improved fodder digestibility. Compare **NaOH**, **urea**, and **lime [Ca(OH)₂]** treatments in terms of mechanism, dosage, treatment time, digestibility improvement, and cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data were obtained from a NaOH pretreatment study on paddy straw (variety *Jyothi*, Kerala KAU):

| Parameter | Raw | NaOH Treated |
|---|---|---|
| NDF (%) | 72.0 | 58.5 |
| ADF (%) | 48.0 | 39.0 |
| *In vitro* DM digestibility (%) | 42.0 | 67.5 |
| Crude protein (%) | 3.5 | 3.2 |

  (i) Calculate the **hemicellulose** content of raw and treated straw. Comment on the change.
  (ii) Calculate the **% improvement in digestibility**.
  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Chemical Pretreatment Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | NaOH (Sodium Hydroxide) | Urea | Lime Ca(OH)2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Dissolves silica, cleaves ester & ether lignin–carbohydrate bonds | Releases NH₃, mild alkaline swelling, adds NPN | Disrupts lignin seal via Ca²⁺ cross-link cleavage |
| **Dosage** | 4–8 % w/w of straw | 4–5 % w/w | 5–10 % w/w |
| **Concentration / Solution** | 6 % NaOH in water | 4 % urea in 50 % moisture | 10 % lime slurry |
| **Treatment Time** | 12–24 h (soaking) or 1 h (spraying + storage) | 21 days (anaerobic storage) | 7–14 days |
| **Temperature** | 25–30 °C (ambient) | 25–30 °C | 25–80 °C (steam possible) |
| **Digestibility Improvement** | 40 % → 65–70 % (Δ ≈ +25–30 %) | 40 % → 55–60 % (Δ ≈ +15–20 %) | 40 % → 58–63 % (Δ ≈ +18–23 %) |
| **Cost (₹/kg straw)** | High (₹0.8–1.2) | Low (₹0.3–0.5) | Very Low (₹0.2–0.4) |
| **Farmer Friendliness** | Low (corrosive) | High (safe) | Moderate (caustic) |
| **Silica Removal** | High (60–80 %) | Low (<20 %) | Moderate (30–50 %) |
| **By-product** | Black liquor (pollution) | Safe effluent | CaCO₃ + sugars |

**Best choice for Kerala small-holder dairy farms:** *Urea treatment* — low cost, no effluent problem, adds NPN for rumen microbes. **Best for industrial-scale energy conversion:** *NaOH* — highest delignification and silica removal.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Hemicellulose content (4 marks):**
By definition, $\text{Hemicellulose} = NDF - ADF$ (since NDF = cellulose + hemicellulose + lignin, and ADF = cellulose + lignin + ash-insoluble).

- Raw straw:
$$
Hemi_{raw} = 72.0 - 48.0 = 24.0 \text{ \%}
$$
- Treated straw:
$$
Hemi_{treated} = 58.5 - 39.0 = 19.5 \text{ \%}
$$
- Change in hemicellulose:
$$
\Delta Hemi = 24.0 - 19.5 = 4.5 \text{ \% (decrease)}
$$
$$
\text{\% loss of hemicellulose} = \dfrac{4.5}{24.0} \times 100 = 18.75 \text{ \%}
$$
**Comment:** NaOH dissolves about 19 % of the hemicellulose fraction, primarily the **xylan** component, into the black liquor. This is the *trade-off* between digestibility gain and carbohydrate loss. **(4 marks)**

**(ii) % improvement in digestibility (3 marks):**
$$
\text{\% improvement} = \dfrac{67.5 - 42.0}{42.0} \times 100 = 60.71 \text{ \%}
$$
$$
\boxed{\text{Improvement} \approx 60.7 \text{ \% relative to raw straw}}
$$
Equivalently, the *absolute* digestibility gain is $67.5 - 42.0 = 25.5$ percentage points, which is a substantial enhancement and confirms NaOH pretreatment is highly effective for tropical paddy straw varieties. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 1: Processing of Paddy Straw — Rapid Recap**

- ✅ **Paddy straw = ligno-cellulosic residue** with cellulose (32–47 %), hemicellulose (19–27 %), lignin (5–24 %), silica (8–16 %).
- ✅ **Bulk density (raw) = 40–120 kg/m³**; pellet density = **600–750 kg/m³** → densification ratio **6–10**.
- ✅ **HHV ≈ 13–16 MJ/kg** (lower than coal 24–30 MJ/kg due to high O & ash).
- ✅ **Four processing domains:** Physical, Chemical, Biological, Thermochemical/Biochemical.
- ✅ **Mechanical densification** uses 80–120 °C, 100–200 MPa; lignin acts as natural binder above its **glass transition temperature (T₉ ≈ 100 °C)**.
- ✅ **NaOH (4–8 %)** is the strongest chemical pretreatment — removes **60–80 % silica** and improves digestibility by **25–30 absolute %**.
- ✅ **Urea (4–5 %, 21 d)** is the **farmer-friendly** alternative for fodder; adds non-protein nitrogen.
- ✅ **Lime [Ca(OH)₂]** is the cheapest pretreatment; moderate delignification; safe in small-scale Kerala dairy farms.
- ✅ **White-rot fungi** (e.g., *Phanerochaete chrysosporium*) selectively degrade lignin via LiP, MnP, laccase — eco-friendly but slow (30 days).
- ✅ **Anaerobic digestion** yields 0.20–0.35 m³ CH₄ per kg VS at 35–55 °C; 20–40 day retention.
- ✅ **Pyrolysis** at 400–600 °C → biochar (30 %) + bio-oil (35 %) + syngas (35 %).
- ✅ **Torrefaction** at 200–300 °C → hydrophobic black pellets with EROI often > 40.
- ✅ **Dulong's HHV formula** (kJ/kg): $338C + 1442(H - O/8) + 927S$ — always convert daf → dry basis using $(1 - \text{Ash}/100)$.
- ✅ **LHV** = HHV − 2260·H − 24.4·MC (accounts for water of combustion + moisture).
- ✅ **Co-firing limit** in Indian thermal power plants: **5–10 % biomass blend** with coal.
- ✅ **C:N ratio** of paddy straw = 60–80 → must co-compost with **nitrogen-rich** substrates (cow dung, biogas slurry) to reach ideal 25–30.
- ✅ **Buswell equation** can predict stoichiometric CH₄/CO₂ yield of any biomass (use for digester design).
- ✅ **Green Kerala context:** Kuttanad and Kole wetlands produce excess straw; the **Kerala State Action Plan on Climate Change (KSPACC)** promotes paddy straw fodder pelletization and biogas pathways as methane-mitigation strategies.
- ✅ **For 14-mark KTU answers,** always include: (i) problem statement (lignin–silica barrier), (ii) flow diagram with by-products, (iii) comparative table of methods, and (iv) numerical example with units + EROI or efficiency metric.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Densification - Extrusion Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT832 - BIOMASS MANAGEMENT FOR FODDER AND ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Densification - Extrusion Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: DENSIFICATION – EXTRUSION PROCESS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Densification** is a thermo-mechanical conversion process in which loose, low-bulk-density lignocellulosic biomass (typically **80–200 kg/m³**) is compressed into uniformly shaped, high-density solid compacts (typically **600–1200 kg/m³**) suitable for storage, transport, and downstream thermochemical or biological utilization.

In the context of **KTU OEAGT832 (Biomass Management for Fodder and Energy)**, densification is a unit operation falling under *Module 1: Biomass Resource Characterization and Pre-processing*. It is the bridge that converts an agriculturally heterogeneous, low-energy-density residue (paddy straw, sawdust, groundnut husk, bagasse, de-oiled cakes) into a standardized, tradeable solid biofuel or feed pellet.

**Extrusion** is one of the three principal mechanical densification routes, alongside **briquetting** (piston/ram type) and **pelletizing** (roller-die type). Specifically, **extrusion densification** is defined as:

> [!NOTE]
> **Extrusion Process (KTU Syllabus Definition):** A continuous densification technique in which biomass material is forced, under the action of a screw, ram, or roller mechanism, through a converging die of defined cross-section. The biomass experiences a **compressive–shear–frictional work input** that overcomes the natural springback of the cellular structure, activates intrinsic binders (lignin, protein, starch), and produces a coherent extrudate of controlled length.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a kitchen garlic press. When you push the handle, a soft clove of garlic is forced through tiny holes and emerges as a continuous, firm noodle. The cell walls of the garlic are crushed, the internal water binds the matrix, and what comes out is denser and structurally continuous.

**Biomass extrusion is the industrial-scale version of that garlic press**, but with three major upgrades:
1. The biomass is **pre-conditioned** (ground to 2–6 mm, moisture adjusted to **8–15 % w.b.**, sometimes steam-conditioned at 80–110 °C).
2. The compressive force is generated by a **rotating screw (or reciprocating ram)** driven by a high-torque motor — not human muscle.
3. The "noodles" that emerge are sliced into **pellets (Ø 6–12 mm)** or **briquettes (Ø 30–80 mm)**, and their density is **5–8 times** the original bulk density.

> [!TIP]
> **Why do we need extrusion for fodder and energy?** A truckload of loose rice straw has an energy density of only ~3 GJ/m³ — the same volume of extruded pellets delivers ~10–12 GJ/m³. This directly cuts logistics cost, storage footprint, and feeder hoppers' bridging problems in gasifiers and boilers.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following KTU-referenced metrics are **bold-marked** for quick revision:

- **Standard pellet diameter:** 6 mm, 8 mm, 10 mm, 12 mm
- **Standard briquette diameter:** 50 mm, 65 mm, 75 mm, 90 mm
- **Bulk density of loose biomass:** **80–200 kg/m³**
- **Density of extruded pellets:** **600–750 kg/m³** (feed-grade), **1100–1300 kg/m³** (fuel-grade)
- **Optimum moisture content for extrusion:** **8–12 % w.b.** for fuel pellets, **10–15 % w.b.** for fodder pellets
- **Die pressure range:** **50–200 MPa** (typical screw), **100–400 MPa** (ram/piston)
- **Throughput range:** **0.2–4.0 t·h⁻¹** depending on die diameter and screw RPM

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Step-Wise Logic of the Extrusion Process

The extrusion process is **not a single event**; it is a sequential, integrated thermo-mechanical chain. For KTU answers, students must articulate each step clearly.

**Step 1 — Reception and Pre-conditioning of Feedstock**
- Raw biomass is received at ~**25–40 % w.b.** moisture (post-harvest condition).
- It is hammer-milled or knife-milled to a **geometric mean particle size of 2–6 mm**.
- Justification: Particles finer than 2 mm consume excess specific energy; particles coarser than 6 mm produce a fractured, low-durability extrudate.

**Step 2 — Drying and Moisture Equalization**
- Moisture is reduced to **8–12 % w.b.** using rotary drum dryers, belt dryers, or pneumatic conveying dryers.
- Excess moisture causes steam flashes at the die face → pellet cracking ("explosion effect").
- Insufficient moisture prevents lignin mobilization and reduces inter-particle bonding.

**Step 3 — Conveying and Compression Zone**
- The screw (or piston) pushes material from the feed throat → compression zone → metering zone.
- In the **compression zone**, material is compacted and air in voids is expelled (volume reduction up to 80 %).
- In the **metering zone**, the compacted mass reaches a pressure exceeding the die-entry resistance.

**Step 4 — Die Formation (Briquetting/Pelletizing Stage)**
- The compacted plug is forced through a converging die.
- **L/D ratio of die (length/diameter):** typically **6:1 to 8:1** for fuel pellets, **3:1 to 5:1** for fodder pellets.
- Lignin softens at **120–160 °C** and acts as a thermoset binder; proteins and starch gelatinize and act as additional binders.

**Step 5 — Cutting and Cooling**
- A rotary knife (or shear blade) cuts the extrudate into uniform lengths.
- Hot pellets (~**80–95 °C**) are cooled in a counter-flow cooler to ambient temperature, during which the lignin hardens and locks the structure.

### 2.2 Why & How — The Bonding Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five bonding mechanisms operate during extrusion:**
> 1. **Solid bridges** — diffusion of lignin, starch, protein molecules across particle boundaries.
> 2. **Adhesion and cohesion forces** — van der Waals and electrostatic attraction at close contact.
> 3. **Mechanical interlocking** — fibers and rough surfaces interlock under compression.
> 4. **Capillary (surface tension) forces** — liquid bridges of water and natural oils.
> 5. **Viscoelastic relaxation suppression** — high die pressure prevents springback.

### 2.3 Types of Extruders Used in Biomass Densification

| Extruder Type | Operating Principle | Typical Pressure (MPa) | Throughput | Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Screw extruder** | Continuous, rotating helical screw | 50–150 | High (0.5–4 t/h) | Sawdust, rice straw, wood shavings |
| **Piston/Ram extruder** | Reciprocating hydraulic/mechanical piston | 100–400 | Medium (0.3–1 t/h) | Cereal straws, sugarcane bagasse |
| **Roller-die pellet mill** | Roller rotates biomass through ring-die | 80–200 | Very high (1–5 t/h) | Compound feed, alfalfa |
| **Pellet press (flat-die)** | Vertical die with rotating rollers | 50–100 | Low–Medium (0.1–0.5 t/h) | Small-scale fodder pellets |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **high-yield, KTU-exam-relevant formulas** for the extrusion process. All equations are mathematically derivable from first principles.

**Table 2.1: Densification & Extrusion Formula Sheet**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Variable Definitions | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_{compact} = \dfrac{m_{pellet}}{V_{pellet}}$ | $m$ = mass, $V$ = volume | kg·m⁻³ | Compact density |
| 2 | $C_{R} = \dfrac{\rho_{compact}}{\rho_{bulk}}$ | $C_R$ = Compression Ratio | dimensionless | Quantifies densification (typical: 4–8) |
| 3 | $E_{s} = \dfrac{P \cdot m_{pellet}}{Q}$ | $E_s$ = Specific energy, $P$ = power, $Q$ = throughput | kWh·t⁻¹ | Energy audit (typical: 30–80 kWh/t) |
| 4 | $\eta_{vol} = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{compact}}$ | $\eta_{vol}$ = Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless | Process efficiency indicator |
| 5 | $P_{die} = \dfrac{4F}{\pi D_{d}^{2}}$ | $P_{die}$ = Die pressure, $F$ = axial force, $D_d$ = die diameter | Pa (MPa) | Die-load design |
| 6 | $M_{db} = \dfrac{100 \cdot M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}}$ | $M_{db}$ = dry-basis moisture, $M_{wb}$ = wet-basis moisture | % | Moisture conversion |
| 7 | $L/D = \dfrac{\text{Die length}}{\text{Die diameter}}$ | $L/D$ = Length-to-diameter ratio | dimensionless | Die geometry selection |
| 8 | $R_{dur} = \dfrac{m_{after}}{m_{before}} \times 100$ | $R_{dur}$ = Pellet durability index | % | Quality (ISO 17831-1; > 95 % = good) |
| 9 | $F_{c} = \dfrac{D_{d}^{2}}{L_{d}} \cdot \Delta P \cdot A_{c}$ | $F_c$ = Compressive force, $L_d$ = die length, $\Delta P$ = pressure drop, $A_c$ = cross-section | N | Force balance on die |
| 10 | $Q = \rho_{compact} \cdot N \cdot V_{m}$ | $Q$ = throughput, $N$ = screw RPM, $V_m$ = volume per screw turn | kg·h⁻¹ | Capacity sizing |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Many students write $C_R$ as $\rho_{bulk}/\rho_{compact}$. The correct definition is **denser ÷ looser** (i.e., $\rho_{compact} / \rho_{bulk}$). Marks are routinely lost on this sign convention.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The extrusion process is the **economic backbone** of:
- **Pellet-fired domestic heating** (Europe: > 18 million tonnes/year consumed).
- **Compound animal feed industry** (India: ~28 million tonnes/year of feed pellets).
- **Co-firing in coal-based thermal power plants** (biomass pellets replacing 5–10 % coal).
- **Torrefied biomass production** (extrusion is the *pre-conditioning* step before torrefaction).
- **Biogas plant feeding** — extruded fodder improves digestibility, increasing methane yield by 10–20 %.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Compression Ratio and Volumetric Efficiency

**Statement:** Show that the compression ratio $C_R$ and the volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol}$ are related by $\eta_{vol} = 1 - 1/C_R$.

**Starting definitions:**

$$C_R = \frac{\rho_{compact}}{\rho_{bulk}}$$

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 - \frac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{compact}}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the reciprocal of the compression ratio.

Since $C_R = \rho_{compact}/\rho_{bulk}$, it follows that:

$$\frac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{compact}} = \frac{1}{C_R}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the volumetric efficiency expression.

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 - \frac{1}{C_R}$$

**Step 3:** Verify numerically with KTU-representative values.

Let $\rho_{compact} = 1100$ kg·m⁻³ and $\rho_{bulk} = 150$ kg·m⁻³.

$$C_R = \frac{1100}{150} = 7.33$$

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 - \frac{1}{7.33} = 1 - 0.1364 = 0.8636 = 86.36\%$$

This means **86.36 %** of the original bulk volume is eliminated during densification.

**Engineering interpretation:** A $C_R$ of 7.33 reduces the storage volume of a 100-tonne lot of rice straw from ~667 m³ to ~91 m³ — a logistics revolution.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Energy Consumption of a Screw Extruder

**Statement:** Derive the specific mechanical energy $E_s$ consumed per tonne of pellets, given motor power $P$ (kW), throughput $Q$ (kg·h⁻¹), and machine efficiency $\eta_m$.

**Step 1:** Define mechanical power delivered to the screw shaft.

$$P_{shaft} = P_{motor} \cdot \eta_{m}$$

where $\eta_m$ typically lies between **0.85 and 0.92** for a gear-coupled extruder.

**Step 2:** Convert throughput to tonnes per hour.

$$Q_{tonne} = \frac{Q_{kg}}{1000}$$

**Step 3:** Specific energy is energy per unit mass:

$$E_s = \frac{P_{shaft}}{Q_{tonne}} = \frac{P_{motor} \cdot \eta_{m} \cdot 1000}{Q_{kg}}$$

**Step 4:** Insert numerical values.

Let $P_{motor} = 90$ kW, $\eta_m = 0.90$, $Q_{kg} = 1500$ kg·h⁻¹.

$$E_s = \frac{90 \times 0.90 \times 1000}{1500} = \frac{81000}{1500} = 54 \text{ kWh·t}^{-1}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with KTU benchmark.

The KTU reference benchmark for wood-pellet extrusion is **30–70 kWh·t⁻¹**. Our result of **54 kWh·t⁻¹** falls comfortably within the acceptable band — the extruder is well-sized.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Die Pressure from Axial Force

**Statement:** Derive the die-entry pressure $P_{die}$ for a circular die of diameter $D_d$ subjected to axial force $F$.

**Step 1:** Area of die entry (circle):

$$A_{die} = \frac{\pi D_{d}^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 2:** Pressure is force per unit area:

$$P_{die} = \frac{F}{A_{die}}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the area expression:

$$P_{die} = \frac{F}{\pi D_{d}^{2}/4} = \frac{4F}{\pi D_{d}^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Numerical evaluation.

Let $F = 31{,}416$ N and $D_d = 20$ mm = $0.020$ m.

$$P_{die} = \frac{4 \times 31416}{\pi \times (0.020)^{2}} = \frac{125664}{0.0012566} = 1.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A 100 MPa die pressure is sufficient to mobilize lignin in softwood biomass (glass-transition temperature reached at this isostatic pressure).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Throughput of a Screw Extruder

**Statement:** Show that the volumetric throughput $Q_v$ of a screw extruder is the product of cross-sectional area, screw pitch, and rotational speed, then convert to mass throughput.

**Step 1:** For a single-flight screw of pitch $p$, the volume swept per revolution in the metering zone is:

$$V_{m} = A_{screw} \cdot p$$

where $A_{screw}$ is the cross-sectional area of the screw channel.

**Step 2:** Volume throughput per unit time:

$$Q_{v} = A_{screw} \cdot p \cdot N \cdot \phi$$

where $N$ is the rotational speed (rev·s⁻¹) and $\phi$ is the **fill factor** (typically 0.3–0.6 for biomass extruders).

**Step 3:** Mass throughput:

$$Q_{m} = \rho_{compact} \cdot Q_{v} = \rho_{compact} \cdot A_{screw} \cdot p \cdot N \cdot \phi$$

**Step 4:** Numerical evaluation.

Let $\rho_{compact} = 1150$ kg·m⁻³, $A_{screw} = 8.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m², $p = 0.045$ m, $N = 1.5$ rev·s⁻¹ (90 RPM), $\phi = 0.45$.

$$Q_{m} = 1150 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.045 \times 1.5 \times 0.45$$

Let us compute step by step:

$$1150 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-3} = 9.775$$

$$9.775 \times 0.045 = 0.4399$$

$$0.4399 \times 1.5 = 0.6598$$

$$0.6598 \times 0.45 = 0.2969 \text{ kg·s}^{-1}$$

Converting to kg·h⁻¹:

$$Q_{m} = 0.2969 \times 3600 = 1069 \text{ kg·h}^{-1} \approx 1.07 \text{ t·h}^{-1}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This is the typical throughput of a **75 kW wood-pellet extruder** with a 110 mm die — matching KTU reference equipment data.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Variables on Pellet Quality

**Table 3.1: Directional Influence of Extrusion Parameters (KTU-Standard)**

| Process Variable | Direction of Increase | Effect on Density | Effect on Durability | Effect on Specific Energy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Moisture content (8–15 %)** | ↑ | ↑ (until optimum, then ↓) | ↑ (until optimum, then ↓) | ↓ (lubrication effect) |
| **Particle size (2–6 mm)** | ↑ (coarser) | ↓ | ↓ (sharply) | ↓ |
| **Die L/D ratio** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ (markedly) | ↑ |
| **Screw RPM** | ↑ | ↓ slightly | ↓ (less residence) | ↓ per hour, ↑ per tonne |
| **Pre-heating temperature (80–110 °C)** | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ |
| **Applied die pressure** | ↑ | ↑ (logarithmic) | ↑ | ↑ |

> [!TIP]
> This table is a **favourite Part-A question** in KTU ESE. Memorize the directional arrows; examiners award full marks only if the rationale (e.g., "↑ RPM → ↓ residence time → insufficient lignin mobilization → ↓ durability") is given.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Screw Extrusion Line

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **end-to-end functional flow** of an industrial biomass extrusion plant, from feedstock reception to bagged pellets. This is the most commonly asked schematic question in KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FEEDSTOCK HOPPER] --> B[HAMMER MILL]
    B --> C[BUCKET ELEVATOR]
    C --> D[ROTARY DRUM DRYER]
    D --> E[SCREW CONVEYOR]
    E --> F[CONDITIONING CHAMBER]
    F --> G[SCREW EXTRUDER MAIN BODY]
    G --> G1[FEED ZONE]
    G1 --> G2[COMPRESSION ZONE]
    G2 --> G3[METERING ZONE]
    G3 --> H[DIE HEAD]
    H --> I[ROTARY CUTTER]
    I --> J[COUNTER FLOW COOLER]
    J --> K[VIBRATORY SCREEN]
    K --> L[FINES RECYCLE TO E]
    K --> M[AUTOMATIC BAGGING UNIT]
    M --> N[WAREHOUSE STORAGE]
```

### Interpretation of the Block Flow

- **A → B**: Raw biomass is fed by gravity / screw feeder into the hammer mill.
- **B → C**: Milled particles are lifted to the dryer hopper.
- **C → D → E**: Moisture is reduced from ~30 % w.b. to ~10 % w.b. in the rotary drum dryer, then conveyed forward.
- **E → F → G**: The conditioned biomass enters the conditioning chamber where live steam (optional) raises the temperature to 80–110 °C — this is the **glass-transition precursor** step.
- **G1 → G2 → G3**: Inside the extruder barrel, the screw drives biomass through three functional zones: feeding, compression, and metering.
- **G3 → H**: The high-pressure plug is forced through the die; lignin softens and binds.
- **H → I**: The continuous extrudate is sliced into pellets by a rotary knife spinning at 800–1500 RPM.
- **I → J**: Hot pellets are cooled to ambient; the binder hardens.
- **J → K**: Fines and broken pellets are separated by vibration.
- **K → L**: Fines are returned to the conditioner (closed-loop recycle).
- **K → M → N**: On-spec pellets are bagged (15–25 kg) or bulk-loaded and dispatched.

## 4.2 Schematic — Cross-Sectional View of a Screw Extruder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BARREL [EXTRUDER BARREL]
        direction LR
        A1[FEED INLET] --> A2[FEED ZONE DEEP CHANNEL]
        A2 --> A3[COMPRESSION ZONE TAPERED CHANNEL]
        A3 --> A4[METERING ZONE SHALLOW CHANNEL]
        A4 --> A5[DIE LAND L/D 6 TO 1]
    end
    subgraph SCREW [HELICAL SCREW SHAFT]
        direction LR
        B1[FEED SECTION DEEP FLIGHT] --> B2[TRANSITION TAPERED FLIGHT]
        B2 --> B3[METERING SHALLOW FLIGHT]
    end
    SCREW --- BARREL
    A5 --> A6[EXIT PELLET TO CUTTER]
```

### Engineering Notes on the Schematic

- The **channel depth** decreases progressively from the feed zone to the metering zone, producing a **compaction ratio of 2:1 to 3:1** at the screw level.
- The die land provides a **steady, controlled pressure drop** that prevents explosive decompression at the die face.
- The barrel is typically **jacketed** to circulate hot water or oil, maintaining wall temperature at 100–150 °C to keep lignin above its glass-transition temperature.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q1.** Define densification. List any **three** advantages of the extrusion process over loose biomass handling.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Densification** is the process of converting loose, low-density lignocellulosic biomass into uniformly shaped, high-density solid compacts (pellets, briquettes, or extrudates) through the application of mechanical pressure, often combined with heat and moisture conditioning.

**Three advantages:**
1. **Increased energy density** — from ~3 GJ/m³ (loose) to ~10–12 GJ/m³ (extruded pellets), reducing transport and storage cost.
2. **Improved handling and flowability** — pellets can be pneumatically conveyed, metered, and fed into boilers/gasifiers without bridging.
3. **Standardized product** — uniform size, moisture (8–12 % w.b.), and calorific value (~16–19 MJ/kg) enable commodity-grade trading.

*(Word limit: ~120 words | Marks: 3)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q2.** Mention the **optimum moisture content** range for fuel pellets and fodder pellets. What happens if moisture content exceeds 18 % w.b.?

**Model Answer:**

- **Fuel pellets:** **8–12 % w.b.**
- **Fodder pellets:** **10–15 % w.b.**

If moisture exceeds 18 % w.b.:
- Water in the die land vaporizes due to compression heating → **steam flash** at the die exit.
- Pellets develop **surface cracks** ("explosion effect").
- Durability index drops below 90 % (ISO 17831-1 failure threshold).
- Spontaneous heating and mould growth in storage.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions for Module 1 carry **14 marks** and typically have an **internal choice**. Two alternative questions are provided below.

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Model]

**Q.A.** (a) Explain the **screw extrusion process** for biomass densification with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the three zones inside the extruder barrel. **(7 marks)**

**(b) A screw extruder produces cylindrical wood pellets of diameter 8 mm and average length 18 mm. The bulk density of sawdust feed is 165 kg/m³ and the pellet density is 1180 kg/m³. Calculate (i) the compression ratio, (ii) the volumetric efficiency, and (iii) the volume of pellets obtained from 1 tonne of loose sawdust. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Process Description:**

The screw extrusion process converts loose biomass into high-density pellets by forcing the material through a die under the combined action of compressive, shear, and frictional forces generated by a rotating helical screw. *(1 mark)*

The functional zones of the extruder barrel are:

1. **Feed Zone** *(2 marks)*
   - Located near the hopper inlet.
   - Has **deep flight channel** to allow high material intake.
   - Conveys biomass forward without significant compression.
   - Acts as a pre-heating zone (jacket heat + friction).

2. **Compression Zone** *(2 marks)*
   - Channel depth **decreases progressively** (tapered flight).
   - Material is compacted; interstitial air is expelled.
   - Bulk density rises sharply; lignin begins to soften.

3. **Metering Zone** *(2 marks)*
   - Has **shallow flight channel** of constant depth.
   - Builds the **final die-entry pressure** (50–150 MPa).
   - Provides uniform plug flow, ensuring pellet consistency.

*(Bonus 1 mark for the block diagram.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $D_p = 8$ mm = $0.008$ m, $L_p = 18$ mm = $0.018$ m, $\rho_{bulk} = 165$ kg/m³, $\rho_{compact} = 1180$ kg/m³, $m_{loose} = 1000$ kg.

**(i) Compression Ratio:** *(2 marks)*

$$C_R = \frac{\rho_{compact}}{\rho_{bulk}} = \frac{1180}{165} = 7.15$$

**(ii) Volumetric Efficiency:** *(2 marks)*

$$\eta_{vol} = 1 - \frac{1}{C_R} = 1 - \frac{1}{7.15} = 1 - 0.1399 = 0.8601 = 86.01\%$$

**(iii) Volume of Pellets from 1 t of Loose Sawdust:** *(3 marks)*

Volume of loose sawdust:

$$V_{loose} = \frac{m_{loose}}{\rho_{bulk}} = \frac{1000}{165} = 6.0606 \text{ m}^3$$

Volume of pellets (mass conserved, assuming negligible moisture loss):

$$V_{pellets} = \frac{m_{loose}}{\rho_{compact}} = \frac{1000}{1180} = 0.8475 \text{ m}^3$$

Verification via $C_R$: $V_{pellets} = V_{loose} / C_R = 6.0606 / 7.15 = 0.8476$ m³ ✓

**Final Answer:** 1 tonne of loose sawdust yields **0.8475 m³** of pellets — a volume reduction of **86 %**.

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Model Alternative]

**Q.B.** (a) Discuss the **bonding mechanisms** active during biomass extrusion. Explain how moisture content and die L/D ratio influence pellet quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b) A piston extruder delivers an axial force of 50 kN on a circular die of diameter 25 mm. The die length is 175 mm. Calculate (i) the die-entry pressure in MPa, (ii) the die L/D ratio, and (iii) the specific energy consumption if the motor draws 18 kW with mechanical efficiency 88 % at a throughput of 320 kg/h. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Bonding Mechanisms & Process Variables:**

**Five bonding mechanisms during extrusion:** *(5 marks, 1 each)*

1. **Solid bridges** — diffusion of lignin, starch, and protein molecules across particle interfaces, locking the matrix after cooling.
2. **Adhesion / cohesion forces** — short-range van der Waals and electrostatic forces activated when inter-particle distance falls below ~1 nm.
3. **Mechanical interlocking** — rough, fibrous particles physically hook into each other under compression.
4. **Capillary (liquid-bridge) forces** — surface tension of water and natural oils holds particles together until the binder solidifies.
5. **Viscoelastic suppression** — high die pressure exceeds the elastic recovery threshold, preventing post-extrusion springback.

**Effect of Moisture Content:** *(1 mark)*

- Below 8 % w.b. → insufficient water for capillary bonding, low durability.
- Optimum 10–12 % w.b. → maximum durability and density.
- Above 15 % w.b. → steam flashes, cracking, post-pellet swelling.

**Effect of Die L/D Ratio:** *(1 mark)*

- Higher L/D (6:1 to 8:1) → longer residence time → more shear work → better lignin mobilization → higher durability and density, but higher specific energy.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $F = 50{,}000$ N, $D_d = 25$ mm = $0.025$ m, $L_d = 175$ mm, $P_{motor} = 18$ kW, $\eta_m = 0.88$, $Q = 320$ kg/h.

**(i) Die-Entry Pressure:** *(2 marks)*

$$P_{die} = \frac{4F}{\pi D_{d}^{2}} = \frac{4 \times 50000}{\pi \times (0.025)^{2}}$$

$$= \frac{200000}{0.0019635} = 1.019 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 101.9 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Die L/D Ratio:** *(2 marks)*

$$L/D = \frac{175}{25} = 7.0$$

This is in the **typical fuel-pellet range of 6:1 to 8:1** — good for high-durability output.

**(iii) Specific Energy Consumption:** *(3 marks)*

$$E_s = \frac{P_{motor} \cdot \eta_m \cdot 1000}{Q} = \frac{18 \times 0.88 \times 1000}{320}$$

$$= \frac{15840}{320} = 49.5 \text{ kWh·t}^{-1}$$

This lies within the **KTU reference band of 30–70 kWh·t⁻¹** — the extruder is energy-efficient.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign convention for $C_R$** — always denser ÷ looser; reversed ratio is a **-1 mark** penalty.
> 2. **Unit conversion for $P_{die}$** — students often leave the answer in Pa instead of MPa. Always convert to MPa for KTU papers.
> 3. **Moisture basis confusion** — distinguish between **wet-basis (w.b.)** and **dry-basis (d.b.)** explicitly in the answer.
> 4. **Forgetting the binding mechanism diagram** — examiners award **1 mark** specifically for a labeled block diagram of bonding mechanisms in Part B (a).
> 5. **Throughput ≠ feed rate** — throughput is the **mass flow at the die exit**, not the screw inlet. State it clearly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Densification & Extrusion Process**

- ☐ Densification increases biomass bulk density from **80–200 kg/m³** to **600–1300 kg/m³** (compression ratio 4–8).
- ☐ Three main densification routes: **briquetting, pelletizing, extrusion**.
- ☐ Extrusion is a **continuous** process using a **screw, ram, or roller** to force biomass through a converging die.
- ☐ Three extruder zones: **feed (deep channel) → compression (tapered) → metering (shallow)**.
- ☐ Optimum moisture for fuel pellets: **8–12 % w.b.**; for fodder: **10–15 % w.b.**
- ☐ Die **L/D ratio**: **6:1 to 8:1** for fuel pellets, **3:1 to 5:1** for fodder.
- ☐ Five bonding mechanisms: solid bridges, adhesion, mechanical interlocking, capillary forces, viscoelastic suppression.
- ☐ **Lignin** is the primary natural binder — activates at **120–160 °C** (glass-transition).
- ☐ Die pressure range: **50–200 MPa** (screw), **100–400 MPa** (ram).
- ☐ Specific energy benchmark: **30–70 kWh·t⁻¹** for wood-pellet extrusion.
- ☐ Key formula: $C_R = \rho_{compact}/\rho_{bulk}$ and $\eta_{vol} = 1 - 1/C_R$.
- ☐ Key formula: $P_{die} = 4F/(\pi D_d^2)$.
- ☐ Key formula: $E_s = P_{motor} \cdot \eta_m \cdot 1000 / Q_{kg/h}$.
- ☐ Pellet durability must exceed **95 %** (ISO 17831-1) for commercial grade.
- ☐ Extrusion reduces storage volume by **~86 %** and transport cost by **~60 %**.
- ☐ Real-world applications: domestic heating pellets, compound feed, co-firing in thermal plants, torrefaction pre-conditioning, biogas digester feedstock.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pellets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAGT832 - BIOMASS MANAGEMENT FOR FODDER AND ENERGY > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Pellets # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pellets** (in the context of biomass management) are **densified, cylindrical solid agglomerates** manufactured by compressing mechanically ground biomass feedstock (lignocellulosic residues, woody biomass, agricultural straw, or fodder/forage material) through a die-press under elevated temperature and pressure, using a binding mechanism that may be intrinsic (lignin plasticization), added (binders), or steam-induced (starch gelatinization). According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme OEAGT832 syllabus** (Module 1: Biomass Resources, Characterization & Densification), pellets are classified as a **secondary solid biofuel** and a **densified form of fodder**, produced to overcome the inherent disadvantages of raw biomass — namely **low bulk density (~40–200 kg/m³)**, **irregular shape**, **high moisture sensitivity**, and **poor handling/transport economics**. The technical standards governing pellets include: - **ISO 17225-2**: Graded wood pellets for industrial/commercial/domestic use. - **ENplus A1 / A2 / B**: European certification for residential-grade pellets. - **IS 17265 (BIS, India)**: Indian standard specification for biomass pellets. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Imagine squeezing a handful of dry grass tightly between your palms.** > The loose grass occupies a large volume and falls apart easily. But when you compress it firmly, friction and natural resins (lignin) glue the strands into a compact, firm, sausage-like lump. **That is, in essence, a biomass pellet** — a mechanically compressed "energy capsule" that packs the calorific value of a large pile of loose biomass into a small, neat, standardized brick. A **fodder pellet** works the same way: ground hay, alfalfa, or straw is forced through a die to produce uniform, easy-to-feed pellets for livestock — reducing waste, improving digestibility, and enabling automated feeding systems. ## 1.3 Key Physical & Engineering Metrics | Metric | Typical Range (Wood Pellets) | Typical Range (Fodder Pellets) | Standard | |--------|------------------------------|---------------------------------|----------| | Diameter | **6 mm / 8 mm / 10 mm** | 6 mm – 12 mm | ISO 17225-2 | | Length | **≤ 40 mm** (typically 3–4 × diameter) | 10 mm – 30 mm | ISO 17829 | | Bulk Density | **650–750 kg/m³** | 600–750 kg/m³ | ISO 17828 | | Moisture Content | **≤ 10 wt%** (A1) / ≤ 12 wt% | ≤ 12 wt% | ISO 18134 | | Calorific Value (NCV) | **≥ 16.5 MJ/kg** (A1) | N/A (feed metric) | ISO 18125 | | Mechanical Durability | **≥ 97.5%** (A1) | ≥ 90% | ISO 17831-1 | | Fines (< 3.15 mm) | **≤ 1.0 wt%** | ≤ 5 wt% | ISO 18846 | > [!NOTE] > **Why these numbers matter:** A standard 6 mm wood pellet has roughly **10× the bulk density** of sawdust. This means **10 truckloads of sawdust = 1 truckload of pellets**, dramatically cutting transport cost per GJ of delivered energy. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Pelletization — The Density Problem Raw biomass suffers from three critical engineering deficiencies: 1. **Low Energy Density (volumetric):** Sawdust ≈ 4–6 GJ/m³; Wood Pellets ≈ 10–12 GJ/m³; Coal ≈ 25–30 GJ/m³. 2. **Hygroscopic Behaviour:** Loose biomass reabsorbs moisture → fungal decay, spontaneous combustion (biological self-heating). 3. **Handling Losses:** Fines generation, dust explosions (Kst > 200 bar·m/s for some agricultural dusts), irregular flow in hoppers. > **The "Why":** Pelletization converts a problematic **low-density, high-surface-area, hygroscopic** material into a **high-density, low-surface-area, water-resistant** standardized commodity — making biomass tradeable on global energy markets (e.g., CIF ARA coal-equivalent pricing). ## 2.2 The "How" — Operational Steps of Pelletization The densification process exploits the **glass-transition temperature** of lignin (Tg ≈ 100–135 °C depending on moisture and species). Above Tg, lignin becomes plastic and acts as a **natural binder**, welding adjacent particles upon cooling. **Process Flow Logic:** - **Step 1: Size Reduction** — Hammer mill or knife mill reduces feedstock to ≤ 4 mm particle size (critical for die throughput). - **Step 2: Drying** — Moisture reduced from 40–60% to **10–12%** using rotary drum or belt dryers (excess moisture ⇒ steam flash in die; insufficient ⇒ cracking). - **Step 3: Conditioning** — Steam injection at **80–90 °C** pre-heats mash, activates natural binders (lignin + starch), and lubricates die wall. - **Step 4: Pelletizing (Die Press)** — Material is forced by **roller(s)** through tapered die channels under pressure of **80–250 MPa**. - **Step 5: Cooling & Stabilization** — Hot pellets (≈ 80–95 °C) are counter-flow cooled with ambient air to ≈ 25 °C; moisture migrates outward; pellet hardens. - **Step 6: Screening** — Fines (< 3.15 mm) are removed and recycled to pelletizer. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **all key engineering formulas** required for KTU examinations on pelletization. Memorize these for guaranteed marks in numerical problems. | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|--------------------|---------|---------------| | 1 | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m_{pellets}}{V_{container}}$ | Bulk density | kg/m³ | | 2 | $DU = \dfrac{m_{retained}}{m_{initial}} \times 100$ | Mechanical Durability (Pfost method) | % | | 3 | $NCV_{daf} = GCV - h \cdot (9H)$ | Net Calorific Value (dry ash-free) | MJ/kg | | 4 | $E_{vol} = \rho_b \cdot NCV$ | Volumetric Energy Density | GJ/m³ | | 5 | $\eta_{press} = \dfrac{m_{pellets,out}}{m_{feed,in}} \times 100$ | Press Efficiency / Throughput Yield | % | | 6 | $P_{req} = \dfrac{F_{comp} \cdot v_{ram}}{\eta_{mech}}$ | Power requirement for pellet mill | kW | | 7 | $F_{comp} = p_{die} \cdot A_{ram}$ | Compression force from die pressure | N | | 8 | $T_{g,L} = T_{g,dry} - k \cdot M_{db}$ | Glass transition temp of lignin (Gordon effect) | °C | | 9 | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry}} \times 100$ | Moisture Content (dry basis) | % | | 10 | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{wet}} \times 100$ | Moisture Content (wet basis) | % | | 11 | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}/100}$ | Conversion db ↔ wb | — | | 12 | $Fines\_Ratio = \dfrac{m_{<3.15mm}}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | Fines percentage (quality metric) | % | > **Where:** > - $h$ = hydrogen mass fraction in fuel > - $9H$ = latent heat of vaporization of formed water (~9 MJ per kg H) > - $T_{g,dry}$ ≈ 135–220 °C; $k$ ≈ 5–7 °C per % moisture > - $p_{die}$ = typical 80–250 MPa in flat-die / ring-die presses ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Co-firing in Coal Power Plants:** Pellet substitution up to 10–15% by mass in existing PC-fired boilers without major retrofit. 2. **Export Commodity:** Wood pellets are traded internationally (USA → EU, Canada → Japan) as **"drop-in" renewable fuel**. 3. **Livestock Productivity:** Fodder pellets ensure **balanced TDN (Total Digestible Nutrients)** intake, reduce feed sorting, and improve feed conversion ratio (FCR) by 8–15%. 4. **Carbon Accounting:** Pellets qualify under **EU RED II** and **CCTS (India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme, 2024)** for renewable energy certificates. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass Balance on a Rotary Drum Dryer (Pre-Pelletizer) **Problem Setup:** Sawdust enters at $M_1$ kg/h with wet-basis moisture $w_1$ = 55%. It must be dried to $w_2$ = 11% before pelletizing. Determine the **dry solid mass flow** and **evaporated water mass flow**. ### Step 1 — Define Moisture Content Conversions (db) Convert wet-basis to dry-basis (because mass balance on dry solid is independent of moisture). $$w_{db,1} = \dfrac{w_{wb,1}}{1 - w_{wb,1}/100} = \dfrac{0.55}{1 - 0.55} = \dfrac{0.55}{0.45}$$ $$w_{db,1} = 1.2222 \; \text{kg water / kg dry solid}$$ $$w_{db,2} = \dfrac{w_{wb,2}}{1 - w_{wb,2}/100} = \dfrac{0.11}{1 - 0.11} = \dfrac{0.11}{0.89}$$ $$w_{db,2} = 0.1236 \; \text{kg water / kg dry solid}$$ > **Explanation:** Dry basis expresses moisture per unit of dry solid (a conserved quantity through the dryer), unlike wet basis which uses total mass. ### Step 2 — Identify the Conserved Quantity In a dryer with no solid loss, **dry solid mass flow is conserved**. $$\dot{m}_{dry} = \dot{m}_{feed} \cdot (1 - w_{wb,1}) = 1000 \cdot (1 - 0.55)$$ $$\dot{m}_{dry} = 450 \; \text{kg dry solid/h}$$ > **Explanation:** 1000 kg/h of wet sawdust contains 55% water → only 450 kg/h is actually dry biomass that survives drying. ### Step 3 — Write Mass Balance on Water $$\dot{m}_{water,in} + \dot{m}_{evap} = \dot{m}_{water,out}$$ Equivalently, since dry solid is conserved: $$\dot{m}_{water,in} - \dot{m}_{water,out} = \dot{m}_{evap}$$ Water associated with the **same 450 kg/h of dry solid** at inlet vs. outlet: $$\dot{m}_{water,in} = \dot{m}_{dry} \cdot w_{db,1} = 450 \cdot 1.2222 = 550 \; \text{kg/h}$$ $$\dot{m}_{water,out} = \dot{m}_{dry} \cdot w_{db,2} = 450 \cdot 0.1236 = 55.6 \; \text{kg/h}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for Evaporated Water $$\dot{m}_{evap} = 550 - 55.6$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m}_{evap} = 494.4 \; \text{kg water/h}}$$ ### Step 5 — Outlet Mass Flow Verification $$\dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}_{dry} + \dot{m}_{water,out} = 450 + 55.6$$ $$\dot{m}_{out} = 505.6 \; \text{kg dried sawdust/h}$$ > **Check moisture:** $55.6 / 505.6 = 0.1100$ ✓ (matches target 11% wb). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Net Calorific Value of Wood Pellets (Dulong-Style Correction) **Given:** Ultimate analysis of pine wood pellet (dry basis): C = 51.2%, H = 6.1%, O = 41.3%, N = 0.4%, S = 0.05%, Ash = 0.95%. All percentages by mass. **Step 1 — Apply Modified Dulong Equation (dry basis)** $$GCV_{dry} = 0.3383 \cdot C + 1.422 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095 \cdot S$$ Substitute values (in %): $$GCV_{dry} = 0.3383 \cdot 51.2 + 1.422 \cdot \left(6.1 - \dfrac{41.3}{8}\right) + 0.095 \cdot 0.05$$ Compute each term: - Carbon term: $0.3383 \cdot 51.2 = 17.32 \; \text{MJ/kg}$ - Hydrogen term: $\dfrac{41.3}{8} = 5.1625$, so $(6.1 - 5.1625) = 0.9375$, then $1.422 \cdot 0.9375 = 1.333 \; \text{MJ/kg}$ - Sulphur term: $0.095 \cdot 0.05 = 0.0048 \; \text{MJ/kg}$ Sum: $$GCV_{dry} = 17.32 + 1.333 + 0.0048$$ $$GCV_{dry} = 18.66 \; \text{MJ/kg (dry basis)}$$ **Step 2 — Convert to Net Calorific Value (NCV, dry)** $$NCV_{dry} = GCV_{dry} - 2.442 \cdot \left(\dfrac{9H}{100}\right)$$ Here the factor 2.442 MJ/kg converts $9H$ (hydrogen-bound water) at constant pressure; using simplification $2.442 \cdot M_H/100 \cdot 9$ is equivalent to $0.2198 \cdot H$ when H is in mass%. $$NCV_{dry} = 18.66 - 0.2198 \cdot 6.1$$ $$NCV_{dry} = 18.66 - 1.341$$ $$NCV_{dry} = 17.32 \; \text{MJ/kg (dry basis)}$$ **Step 3 — Correct for Pellet Moisture (as-fired, wb = 10%)** $$NCV_{as\text{-}fired} = NCV_{dry} \cdot (1 - w_{wb}) - 2.442 \cdot w_{wb}$$ $$NCV_{as\text{-}fired} = 17.32 \cdot (1 - 0.10) - 2.442 \cdot 0.10$$ $$NCV_{as\text{-}fired} = 15.588 - 0.2442$$ $$\boxed{NCV_{as\text{-}fired} = 15.34 \; \text{MJ/kg}}$$ > **Interpretation:** A 10% moisture pellet loses ~1.98 MJ/kg from its theoretical dry NCV — a critical reason why **dryer efficiency directly translates into pellet market value** (every 1% moisture ≈ ₹150–250/tonne loss at current biomass CIF prices). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Volumetric Energy Density Comparison **Step 1 — Compute pellet volumetric energy density** $$E_{vol,pellet} = \rho_{b,pellet} \cdot NCV_{pellet}$$ $$E_{vol,pellet} = 700 \; \text{kg/m}^3 \cdot 17.32 \; \text{MJ/kg}$$ $$E_{vol,pellet} = 12{,}124 \; \text{MJ/m}^3 = 12.12 \; \text{GJ/m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Compute sawdust volumetric energy density** $$E_{vol,sawdust} = \rho_{b,sawdust} \cdot NCV_{sawdust}$$ Using $\rho_{b,sawdust} \approx 180$ kg/m³ and same NCV (composition identical, only densification differs): $$E_{vol,sawdust} = 180 \cdot 17.32 = 3{,}118 \; \text{MJ/m}^3 = 3.12 \; \text{GJ/m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Compute the densification ratio** $$R_{densify} = \dfrac{E_{vol,pellet}}{E_{vol,sawdust}} = \dfrac{12.12}{3.12}$$ $$\boxed{R_{densify} \approx 3.89}$$ > **Interpretation:** Pellets deliver **≈ 3.9 times more energy per cubic metre** of storage/transport volume than loose sawdust. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Flow Architecture — Biomass Pellet Manufacturing Line > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagram below maps the **end-to-end industrial pellet manufacturing flow** — from raw biomass intake to bagged finished pellets. Each node represents a unit operation with its key control parameters. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Biomass Intake
Sawdust / Straw / Bagasse] --> B[Step 1 Hammer Mill
Particle Size Reduction] B --> C{Step 2 Drying
Moisture 55% to 11%} C -->|Hot Air 250 C| D[Step 3 Cyclone Separator
Dust Collection] D --> E[Step 4 Conditioning Chamber
Steam Injection 80 to 90 C] E --> F[Step 5 Pellet Press
Ring Die or Flat Die
Pressure 80 to 250 MPa] F --> G[Step 6 Counter Flow Cooler
Pellet T 90 C to 25 C] G --> H[Step 7 Vibrating Screener
Fines Removal < 3.15 mm] H --> I[Step 8 Bagging and Palletizing
Bulk or 25 kg Bags] H -->|Recycle Fines| E I --> J[Finished Pellets
ENplus A1 or A2 Certified] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Pellet Quality Control System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT [Feedstock Variables] I1[Moisture Content] I2[Particle Size Distribution] I3[Lignin Content] I4[Ash Content] end subgraph PROCESS [Pelletizer Process Variables] P1[Die Temperature] P2[Die Compression Force] P3[Throughput Rate] P4[Steam Conditioning Time] end subgraph OUTPUT [Pellet Quality Outputs] O1[Bulk Density] O2[Mechanical Durability] O3[Fines Fraction] O4[Calorific Value] end I1 --> P1 I2 --> P2 I3 --> P1 I4 --> O4 P1 --> O2 P2 --> O1 P3 --> O3 P4 --> O2 P1 --> O1 ``` ## 4.3 Cause-Effect Diagram — Factors Affecting Pellet Durability ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Low Pellet Mechanical Durability] --> M1[Moisture Too High] R --> M2[Moisture Too Low] R --> M3[Coarse Particle Size] R --> M4[Insufficient Lignin Activation] R --> M5[Excessive Throughput] R --> M6[Die Wear] R --> M7[Improper Cooling] M1 --> R M2 --> R M3 --> R M4 --> R M5 --> R M6 --> R M7 --> R ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **Define biomass pellets. List any four key quality parameters specified by ENplus / ISO 17225-2 standards.** **Model Answer:** > **Definition:** Biomass pellets are densified cylindrical solid agglomerates of mechanically compressed organic matter (wood, agro-residue, or forage), typically 6–10 mm in diameter and ≤ 40 mm long, produced through a die-press at elevated temperature and pressure. **Four key quality parameters (ENplus A1):** | # | Parameter | ENplus A1 Limit | |---|-----------|-----------------| | 1 | Diameter | 6 mm or 8 mm | | 2 | Moisture Content | ≤ 10 wt% | | 3 | Mechanical Durability | ≥ 97.5% | | 4 | Net Calorific Value | ≥ 16.5 MJ/kg | **Bulk density**, **ash content (≤ 0.7%)**, and **fines (< 1.0%)** are additional quality benchmarks. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]` **Explain the role of lignin in biomass pelletization. What is the typical glass transition temperature (Tg) range of lignin?** **Model Answer:** Lignin is a **natural thermoplastic binder** present in lignocellulosic biomass (15–35% by dry mass). Above its glass transition temperature (Tg), lignin softens and flows into the void spaces between adjacent particles, forming **intermolecular bonds and solid bridges** upon cooling — this is the **primary binding mechanism** in wood pelletization (no external binder needed for woody biomass). The Tg of dry lignin is **approximately 127–220 °C**, but is **strongly reduced by moisture** (plasticization effect, sometimes called the **Gordon effect**): $$T_g \approx T_{g,dry} - k \cdot MC_{db}$$ with $k \approx 5-7$ °C per % db moisture. At 10% moisture, the effective Tg drops to roughly **80–100 °C**, which is why **steam conditioning at 80–90 °C is sufficient** to activate lignin binding in industrial pellet mills. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal sub-parts of **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### **Part B – Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` #### **(a)** Describe the complete manufacturing process of wood pellets from sawdust with a neat process flow diagram. Explain the role of each unit operation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The manufacturing of wood pellets from sawdust proceeds through **six sequential unit operations**: **1. Size Reduction (Hammer Mill):** Sawdust is typically pre-sized but may require further reduction to ≤ 4 mm. Optimal particle size distribution improves die throughput and pellet quality. Fine particles (< 1 mm) favour higher durability. **2. Drying (Rotary Drum / Belt Dryer):** Reduces moisture from 50–60% (wb) to 10–12% (wb). Combustion of wood dust or biogas heats air to 200–300 °C, which flows counter-current to biomass. **Excess moisture causes steam explosions in the die; insufficient moisture prevents lignin activation.** **3. Conditioning (Steam Injection Mixer):** Superheated steam at 0.5–2.0 bar is injected to bring mash temperature to 80–90 °C. This **lubricates the die**, pre-heats the material above lignin's effective Tg, and gelatinizes starch (for agro-pellets). **4. Pelletizing (Ring-Die or Flat-Die Press):** Conditioned mash is forced by rollers through tapered die channels. Compression pressures of **80–250 MPa** elevate temperature further (adiabatic heating), fully activating lignin. Throughput is governed by die geometry, rotational speed, and feedstock characteristics. **5. Cooling (Counter-Flow Cooler):** Hot pellets (80–95 °C, 12–14% moisture) are cooled by ambient air flowing opposite to pellet flow. Moisture migrates outward and evaporates; pellets harden as lignin re-solidifies. Final moisture: 8–10%. **6. Screening and Bagging:** Vibrating screens remove fines (< 3.15 mm), which are recycled to the conditioner. Finished pellets are bagged (15 kg, 25 kg, 500 kg big bags) or bulk-loaded. **Process Flow Diagram (text description):** Sawdust → Hammer Mill → Dryer → Conditioner → Pellet Mill → Cooler → Screener → Bagging → Storage. **(Full 7 marks: 1 mark for process sequence, 3 marks for role of each unit, 2 marks for diagram, 1 mark for parameters.)** --- #### **(b)** A pellet plant processes **1500 kg/h** of wet sawdust at **55% moisture (wb)**. The dried output has **10% moisture (wb)**. Calculate: **(i)** the dry solid flow rate, **(ii)** the moisture to be evaporated, and **(iii)** the heat required in the dryer if the evaporation latent heat is **2400 kJ/kg water** and the biomass is heated from **25 °C to 80 °C** ($c_{p,bio} = 1.9$ kJ/kg·K). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Dry solid flow rate:** $$\dot{m}_{dry} = 1500 \cdot (1 - 0.55) = 675 \; \text{kg dry solid/h}$$ **(ii) Moisture to be evaporated:** Convert wb to db: - $w_{db,in} = 0.55 / (1 - 0.55) = 1.2222$ kg water/kg dry - $w_{db,out} = 0.10 / (1 - 0.10) = 0.1111$ kg water/kg dry $$\dot{m}_{water,in} = 675 \cdot 1.2222 = 825 \; \text{kg/h}$$ $$\dot{m}_{water,out} = 675 \cdot 0.1111 = 75 \; \text{kg/h}$$ $$\dot{m}_{evap} = 825 - 75 = \boxed{750 \; \text{kg water/h}}$$ **(iii) Heat Required:** Component 1 — Sensible heat of biomass: $$Q_{sens} = \dot{m}_{dry} \cdot c_{p,bio} \cdot \Delta T = 675 \cdot 1.9 \cdot (80 - 25)$$ $$Q_{sens} = 675 \cdot 1.9 \cdot 55 = 70{,}612.5 \; \text{kJ/h}$$ Component 2 — Latent heat of evaporation: $$Q_{lat} = \dot{m}_{evap} \cdot h_{fg} = 750 \cdot 2400 = 1{,}800{,}000 \; \text{kJ/h}$$ Total heat (assuming water pre-heated from 25 °C to 80 °C, $c_{p,water} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K): $$Q_{water,sens} = 750 \cdot 4.18 \cdot (80 - 25) = 172{,}425 \; \text{kJ/h}$$ $$\dot{Q}_{total} = 70{,}612.5 + 1{,}800{,}000 + 172{,}425 = 2{,}043{,}037.5 \; \text{kJ/h}$$ $$\dot{Q}_{total} \approx 2043 \; \text{MJ/h} = 567.5 \; \text{kW}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{total} \approx 568 \; \text{kW}}$$ **(Full 7 marks: 2 marks each for parts (i) and (ii), 3 marks for part (iii) with proper step-by-step calculation.)** --- ### **Part B – Question B** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` #### **(a)** Compare biomass pellets with raw biomass (sawdust/agri-residue) on the basis of (i) bulk density, (ii) moisture content, (iii) calorific value, (iv) transportation cost per GJ, and (v) storage life. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** | # | Property | Raw Biomass (Sawdust) | Biomass Pellets | Improvement Factor | |---|----------|----------------------|------------------|--------------------| | (i) | Bulk Density | 150–250 kg/m³ | **650–750 kg/m³** | **≈ 3.5–4×** | | (ii) | Moisture Content | 40–60% (wb) | **≤ 10–12%** (wb) | **≈ 5× lower** | | (iii) | Net Calorific Value | 7–12 MJ/kg (as-fired wet) | **16–18 MJ/kg** | **≈ 1.5–2×** | | (iv) | Transport Cost per GJ | ₹180–280 /GJ | **₹45–70 /GJ** | **≈ 4× cheaper** | | (v) | Storage Life | 3–6 months (mould risk) | **12–24 months** (low moisture) | **≈ 3–4× longer** | **Key Engineering Inferences:** 1. **Bulk Density Increase:** Reduces transport volume by ~70%; enables pneumatic conveying and silo storage without bridging. 2. **Moisture Reduction:** Eliminates biological self-heating (fungal action at MC > 20%), suppresses spontaneous combustion (typically below 10% is safe). 3. **NCV Gain:** From ~9 MJ/kg (wet sawdust) to ~17 MJ/kg (pellet) — directly comparable to low-grade coal. 4. **Cost Economics:** A truck carrying 25 tonnes of pellets delivers ~425 GJ, versus ~85 GJ for the same truck of sawdust. **Per-GJ freight drops by ≈ 80%.** 5. **Storage Stability:** Hydrophobic surface, low fines, standardized dimensions allow **covered, silo-stored bulk piles up to 6 m height** without caking. **(Full 7 marks: 1 mark per row + 2 marks for inference/explanation.)** --- #### **(b)** Discuss the various **binding mechanisms** operative during biomass pelletization. How does feedstock composition affect pellet quality? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** Pellet strength arises from **five coexisting binding mechanisms**, each dominant in different conditions: **1. Solid Bridges (Strongest):** At die temperatures > Tg of lignin (~80–100 °C in moist biomass), lignin softens, flows between particles, and re-solidifies upon cooling, forming strong solid bridges. **Dominant in woody biomass pelletization.** This is the *primary* mechanism for high-durability pellets. **2. Adhesion and Cohesion Forces:** Van der Waals forces and electrostatic attraction between particles contribute at the molecular scale; significant at high compression pressures. **3. Mechanical Interlocking:** Fibrous particles (e.g., straw, hay) physically hook into one another like Velcro. **Important for fodder pellets and agricultural residue pellets** with high cellulose content. **4. Capillary (Surface Tension) Forces:** Moisture present at particle surfaces forms liquid bridges during compaction (effective in 8–15% moisture range). On drying, these bridges leave behind solid bonds due to dissolved sugars and extractives. **5. Chemical Bonds (Minor):** At very high die temperatures (> 200 °C), Maillard-type reactions and partial hemicellulose degradation can form covalent cross-links, but excessive temperature causes **pellet charring and off-gas (CO, NOx)** — undesirable. **Effect of Feedstock Composition:** | Component | Effect on Pellet Quality | |-----------|--------------------------| | Lignin (15–35%) | **Positive:** Acts as natural thermoplastic binder; higher lignin ⇒ higher durability. | | Cellulose/Hemicellulose | **Positive:** Provides fibrous backbone and capillary binding (especially at moderate MC). | | Extractives (fats, waxes, sugars) | **Mixed:** Improve lubrication but can cause die fouling; reduce storage stability. | | Ash (especially K, Cl, Si) | **Negative:** Reduces binding; abrasive to die; lowers fusion tendency. | | Crude Protein (fodder) | **Positive:** Plasticizes at 70–90 °C; aids binding in feed pellets. | **Practical implications:** - **Wood (high lignin):** Self-binding; durable pellets without additives. - **Agro-residues (rice straw, bagasse):** Low lignin, high ash → require **binders** (e.g., starch 2–5%, molasses, or lignin-sulfonate from paper industry). - **Fodder:** Steam conditioning at 80 °C gelatinizes starch (70–80 °C gelatinization) — providing strong binding for feed pellets. **(Full 7 marks: 4 marks for binding mechanisms, 3 marks for feedstock effects.)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing wet-basis vs. dry-basis moisture** in mass balance problems → 2–3 mark loss. Always convert consistently. > 2. **Forgetting to include sensible heat of biomass AND water** in dryer energy balance → loses 1–2 marks. Enumerate ALL heat sinks. > 3. **Writing "glass transition temperature of biomass" instead of "lignin"** → incorrect; biomass is a composite, lignin is the glassy polymer. > 4. **Saying pellets have the same calorific value as sawdust** → wrong. Densification increases **volumetric** energy density, not gravimetric (NCV per kg is similar but as-fired NCV differs due to moisture). > 5. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions** → −2 marks minimum. Always include a process flow diagram. > 6. **Ignoring internal choice clarity** → Read the question paper carefully; answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (not both). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pellets (Module 1, OEAGT832)** - ✅ **Pellets = densified cylindrical biomass agglomerates**, 6–10 mm diameter, ≤ 40 mm length. - ✅ **Primary function:** Solve the **low bulk density, high moisture, irregular shape** problems of raw biomass. - ✅ **Key process:** Size reduction → Drying (≤ 12% MC) → Steam conditioning (80–90 °C) → Die pressing (80–250 MPa) → Cooling → Screening. - ✅ **Binding mechanism:** **Lignin plasticization** (glass transition at ~80–100 °C in moist biomass) is the primary natural binder for woody pellets. - ✅ **Quality standards:** **ENplus A1 / ISO 17225-2** — diameter, length, MC ≤ 10%, NCV ≥ 16.5 MJ/kg, durability ≥ 97.5%. - ✅ **Volumetric energy density gain:** **≈ 3.9×** over loose sawdust; transport cost per GJ drops ~80%. - ✅ **Fodder pellets:** Use **starch gelatinization** (70–80 °C) + **mechanical interlocking** of fibrous particles. - ✅ **Agro-residue pellets:** Low lignin → require **binders** (starch, molasses, lignin-sulfonate). - ✅ **Mass balance:** Always use **dry-basis moisture** for dryers; convert wb ↔ db via $w_{db} = w_{wb}/(1-w_{wb})$. - ✅ **Calorific value:** Use **Modified Dulong** for GCV, subtract **2.442 × w_wb** (sensible) **+ 2.442 × 9H/100** for NCV. - ✅ **Die pressure** ranges: Flat-die 80–150 MPa, Ring-die 150–250 MPa; throughput proportional to die channel area × rotational speed. - ✅ **Fines (< 3.15 mm)** are recycled to conditioner to avoid material loss and dust explosion hazards. - ✅ **Cooling** is essential: hot pellets are soft and high in moisture; cooling to 25 °C allows lignin re-solidification and moisture equilibration. - ✅ **Carbon trading:** Pellets qualify under **EU RED II** and India's **CCTS 2024** as renewable solid biofuel.

topics.title: Mills and Cubes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT832 - BIOMASS MANAGEMENT FOR FODDER AND ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mills and Cubes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mills in Biomass Management — Formal Definition

In the context of **Biomass Management for Fodder and Energy**, **Mills** refer to a broad family of *size-reduction machinery* employed to disintegrate coarse, fibrous, and often lignified biomass (stalks, straw, stover, grass, sugarcane bagasse, woody cuttings, etc.) into smaller, more uniform particles. The reduction in particle size dramatically increases the **surface-area-to-volume ratio**, which in turn accelerates drying, improves digestibility in ruminants, enhances compaction behaviour, and raises the calorific efficiency during combustion or gasification.

From the KTU 2024 Scheme perspective, "mills" under Module 1 typically include:

- **Chaff Cutters** (also called *forage cutters* or *straw choppers*) — the primary fodder-preparation machine.
- **Hammer Mills** — impact-based grinders used for producing meal/flour from dried biomass.
- **Roller / Cracker Mills** — used to *crack* grains or *flake* biomass by compression and shearing.
- **Sieve-integrated Grinders** — combine milling and classification in a single unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Within *Mills and Cubes*, the emphasis is on **Chaff Cutters** (a *cutting-type mill*) and the corresponding **Cuber/Briquetters** that convert the cut material into dense, transportable solid fuel/livestock-feed blocks.

## 1.2 Cubes (and Briquettes) — Formal Definition

A **Cube** in fodder-and-energy terminology is a *densified, geometrically regular* biomass agglomerate produced by applying high mechanical pressure to milled biomass inside a closed die. The compression overcomes the **natural spring-back (relaxation)** of the fibres and creates **inter-particle bonds** (mechanical interlocking, van der Waals forces, and—when heat/steam is added—lignin-softened bridges). The resulting product is called variously a **cube** (square cross-section, ~30 × 30 mm), a **briquette** (larger pillow or cylindrical block), or a **pellet** (small-diameter cylindrical, ~6–12 mm).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Tea Bag Story":**
> Imagine you have a pile of loose tea leaves. If you try to steep them directly in hot water, you get a messy, slow extraction. But if you compress them into a small tea bag (a *cube*), the surface area per unit volume is controlled, the water flows predictably through the matrix, and you can carry the entire day's worth of tea neatly in your pocket.
>
> **Mills** are the act of *tearing the leaves* into smaller flakes (chaff cutting) so that they can be handled.
> **Cubers** are the act of *stuffing those flakes into a tea bag* — making the biomass dense, manageable, and energy-dense.
>
> Without milling, cubing fails (long fibres jam the die).
> Without cubing, milling leaves you with bulky, transport-inefficient biomass.
> **They are two halves of the same densification workflow.**

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Engineering Parameters)

The following metrics define the quality and utility of mill and cube outputs in KTU examinations:

| Metric | Typical Range / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Bulk Density** | Loose: **40–120 kg/m³**; Cubed: **600–750 kg/m³** | Determines storage & transport cost |
| **Particle Length (cut fodder)** | **10–50 mm** | Digestibility index |
| **Cube Cross-Section** | **~30 × 30 mm** | Standard cuber die dimension |
| **Moisture Content (cubable)** | **10–18 % (w.b.)** | Critical for lignin bonding |
| **Throughput (capacity)** | **0.5–5 t/h** (chaff cutter); **1–3 t/h** (cuber) | Commercial viability |
| **Specific Energy Consumption** | **8–25 kWh/t** (milling); **30–60 kWh/t** (cubing) | Operating cost driver |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **6× jump in bulk density** (from ~80 kg/m³ loose to ~650 kg/m³ cubed) is the single most important reason densification is a *pillar* of modern biomass logistics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Mills Used in Fodder/Energy Biomass

Mills are classified by the **dominant comminution mechanism** they employ:

### 2.1.1 Cutting-Type Mills — *The Chaff Cutter*

This is the workhorse of fodder preparation and is the **most-tested mill in KTU papers**.

**Mechanism:** *Shearing* action between a high-speed rotary blade (flywheel-mounted knife) and a stationary shear plate (bed knife).

**Sub-Types:**

1. **Hand-Operated Chaff Cutter**
   - Flywheel rotated by a hand crank via a gear train.
   - Throughput: ~50–100 kg/h. Used by small/marginal farmers.

2. **Power-Operated Chaff Cutter (PTO / Electric Motor)**
   - Flywheel driven by a 1–5 HP motor or tractor PTO.
   - Throughput: 0.5–2 t/h. The standard KTU study item.

3. **Tubular / Flywheel-Type Chaff Cutter**
   - Biomass fed tangentially through a chute; high inertia of flywheel allows cutting of woody stalks up to ~25 mm diameter.

4. **Multi-Cut Forage Harvester**
   - Combines cutting + collection + partial size reduction in one pass.

### 2.1.2 Impact-Type Mills — *Hammer Mill*

**Mechanism:** Free-swinging hammers mounted on a rapidly rotating rotor strike biomass particles against a screen. Particles smaller than the screen aperture exit; oversize is recirculated.

**Use in biomass management:** Producing fine meal from dried stover for **pellet** production or for **biogas-digester feed**.

### 2.1.3 Compression-Type Mills — *Roller Mill / Crusher*

**Mechanism:** Two counter-rotating rollers with corrugations or smooth surfaces apply *compressive + shear* stress. Used to crack maize kernels or flatten oilseeds.

## 2.2 Chaff Cutter — Detailed Operational Breakdown

**Key Components:**

- **Feeding Chute / Hopper** — directs biomass toward the cutting head. Often includes a *feed roller* (power chaff cutters) for uniform feed.
- **Cutting Head (Flywheel + Knives)** — the central spinning disc carrying 2–4 radially mounted knives. The kinetic energy of the flywheel is what actually performs the cut.
- **Stationary (Bed) Knife** — placed tangentially to the flywheel path; provides the counter-edge.
- **Shear Bar / Anvil** — supports the bed knife and provides a small clearance (typically **0.5–1.5 mm**) for clean shearing.
