The compost becomes stable, dark-brown, and earthy-smelling. *Operational Parameters:* | Parameter | Optimum | Effect of Deviation | |---|---|---| | **C : N** | 25 – 30 : 1 | Slow if high; ammonia loss if low | | **Moisture** | 50 – 60 % | Anaerobic if > 65 %; microbial dormancy if < 40 % | | **Temperature** | 55 – 65 °C peak | Pathogen survival if < 50 °C; microbial kill if > 70 °C | | **Aeration (O₂)** | ≥ 5 % | Anaerobic + odor if < 5 %; excessive cooling if > 20 % | | **pH** | 6.5 – 8.0 | Acid stalls if < 5.5; ammonia loss if > 9.0 | | **Particle Size** | 1 – 3 cm | Poor aeration if too large; compaction if too fine | **(b)** Discuss the **role of composting in biomass management for fodder and energy applications**. Mention at least **four specific downstream uses** of finished compost. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Composting plays a **bridging role** in the biomass value chain between organic waste generation and agricultural/energy utilization: 1. **Soil conditioner for fodder fields** — Compost applied at 5 – 10 tonnes/ha improves soil structure, water-holding capacity, and nutrient status, boosting green and dry fodder yield. 2. **Pre-treatment for bioenergy feedstocks** — Composted biomass has **lower moisture** and more uniform composition, improving **briquetting density** and **combustion efficiency** in biomass-to-energy conversion. 3. **Organic fertilizer substitute** — Reduces dependence on chemical NPK, lowering input cost for fodder and energy-crop farmers. Compost supplies **N (1 – 2 %), P (0.5 – 1.5 %), K (1 – 2 %)** in slow-release form. 4. **Carbon sequestration** — Stable humus in compost locks organic carbon into soil for **decades to centuries**, contributing to climate-smart agriculture and biogas/energy-crop sustainability credits. 5. **Carrier for biofertilizers** — Compost acts as a *carrier medium* for *Rhizobium*, *Azotobacter*, and *phosphate-solubilizing bacteria*, enhancing biological nitrogen fixation in fodder legumes. 6. **Livestock mortality management** — Composting of animal carcasses (within coir/lime layers) is a KTUA-recognized biosecure disposal route, preventing disease transmission in fodder-livestock systems. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Mulching & Composting:** > > 1. **C : N Calculation Error** — Students often *invert* the ratio or forget that the numerator/denominator must sum across all substrates. Always write $\dfrac{\sum C_i m_i}{\sum N_i m_i}$ — do NOT just average the C : N ratios. > 2. **Moisture Content Confusion** — Mixing up *wet-basis* and *dry-basis* MC costs 1 – 2 marks per question. Wet-basis is the KTU-standard formula. > 3. **Confusing "Compost" vs "Vermicompost"** — Vermicompost is **mesophilic** (15 – 30 °C), earthworm-mediated, and *cannot* reach thermophilic temperatures; do not claim pathogen-kill at 55 °C in vermicomposting. > 4. **Mulch Layer Thickness** — Stating "10 cm" without a range loses marks; quote **5 – 10 cm** and mention that > 10 cm causes anaerobicity and stem rot. > 5. **Skipping Units** — Always state units (kg, %, °C, day⁻¹); KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit. > 6. **No Mention of Turnover** — Aerobic composting requires periodic turning; failing to mention it = 1-mark loss in 7-mark questions. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Mulching & Composting** - **Mulching Definition:** Covering soil with organic/inorganic layer; thickness **5 – 10 cm**. - **Mulch Benefits (M-W-T-E-N):** Moisture retention, Weed suppression, Temperature buffer, Erosion control, Nutrient release. - **Organic Mulch Examples:** Paddy straw, sugarcane trash, coconut leaves, sawdust, coir pith, neem cake. - **Composting Definition:** Controlled microbial oxidation of organic matter → humus + CO₂ + H₂O + heat. - **Compost Process:** Mesophilic → Thermophilic → Cooling → Maturation. - **Thermophilic Window:** **55 – 65 °C for 3 days** → kills pathogens and weed seeds. - **C : N Ratio Optimum:** **25 : 1 to 30 : 1** (start), **< 20 : 1** (finished). - **Moisture:** **50 – 60 %** wet-basis (use the formula $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$). - **pH Range:** **6.5 – 8.0** during active composting. - **Aeration (O₂):** **≥ 5 %**; turn every 3 – 5 days. - **Composting Types:** Aerobic, Anaerobic, Vermicomposting. - **Vermicomposting Agent:** *Eisenia foetida*; mesophilic; cannot exceed 35 °C. - **Volume Reduction in Composting:** typically **40 – 60 %** of input volume. - **Compost Use in Fodder:** Soil amendment, biofertilizer carrier, erosion control, pre-treatment for bioenergy. - **Mulch Use in Fodder:** Moisture saving (30 – 50 %), weed control, soil temperature regulation. - **C : N Bulk Mix Formula:** $\dfrac{\sum C_i m_i}{\sum N_i m_i}$ — sum numerators and denominators separately. - **Moisture Addition Formula:** $W_{add} = \dfrac{m_{dry} \cdot MC_{tgt}}{1 - MC_{tgt}} - W_{current}$. - **Quality Indicators of Finished Compost:** dark brown, earthy smell, C : N < 20, germination index > 80 %, moisture 20 – 30 %.

topics.title: Paper and Cardboard Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT832 - BIOMASS MANAGEMENT FOR FODDER AND ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Paper and Cardboard Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Paper** is a sheet material made from **cellulose fibers** (primarily obtained from wood, agricultural residues like bagasse, straw, or recycled paper) that are matted, felted, and dried into flexible sheets. **Cardboard** is a heavier, thicker, and more rigid form of paper-based material, typically with a higher basis weight (above **224 g/m²**) and often constructed in **multiple plies or corrugated layers**.

In the context of **biomass management (OEAGT832 Module 4)**, paper and cardboard manufacturing represents one of the most important **industrial valorization pathways** for lignocellulosic biomass. The process converts low-value agricultural residues (bagasse, rice straw, wheat straw, hemp, bamboo) and forestry biomass into high-value commodity products that are **renewable, recyclable, and biodegradable**.

### Standard Industrial Metrics Used in KTU Curriculum

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Basis weight (grammage) | $g$ | 40 – 300 (paper) | g/m² |
| Caliper (thickness) | $t$ | 0.04 – 0.50 (paper) | mm |
| Bursting strength | $B$ | 20 – 600 | kPa |
| Tear resistance | $T$ | 200 – 1500 | mN |
| Tensile index | $I_T$ | 20 – 100 | N·m/g |
| Burst index | $I_B$ | 1 – 8 | kPa·m²/g |
| Canadian Standard Freeness | CSF | 100 – 800 | mL |
| Sheet moisture content | $M_c$ | 4 – 8 | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> Imagine you are making **roti (Indian flatbread)** at home. First, you take wheat (raw biomass), clean it, soak it (pulping), grind it into a soft dough (fiber suspension), roll it out thin on a hot tawa (forming on a wire mesh), and finally dry it to a crisp sheet (drying section).
> **Paper making is the industrial, continuous version of this exact process** — except instead of wheat dough, you use cellulose fibers suspended in water (called "stock"), and instead of a tawa, you use a moving wire mesh (the **Fourdrinier machine**) where water drains away, leaving a wet mat that is pressed, dried, and calendared into a finished sheet.
> For **cardboard**, simply imagine stacking 3–7 such "rotis" together, often with a wavy middle layer (corrugation) — exactly like a sandwich — to give the final product strength and rigidity.

## 1.2 Classification of Paper and Cardboard Products

### A. By Basis Weight
- **Paper:** $g < 224$ g/m² (writing, printing, tissue)
- **Paperboard / Cardboard:** $g \geq 224$ g/m² (packaging, boxes)

### B. By Raw Material
- **Wood-based:** Softwood (long fibers, *Pinus*, *Spruce*) + Hardwood (short fibers, *Eucalyptus*, *Acacia*)
- **Non-wood (agro-based):** Bagasse, rice straw, wheat straw, bamboo, hemp, kenaf
- **Recycled:** Old Corrugated Containers (OCC), Mixed Office Waste (MOW), Deinked Pulp (DIP)

### C. By End Use (KTU-relevant categories)
- **Cultural/Graphic:** Newsprint, copy paper, magazine paper
- **Packaging:** Kraft paper, liner board, corrugating medium
- **Tissue:** Toilet paper, tissue towels
- **Specialty:** Filter paper, currency paper, electrical insulation paper

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Sequential Stages of Paper Manufacturing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Paper and cardboard manufacturing is universally described as a **5-stage sequential pipeline**: **(1) Raw Material Preparation → (2) Pulping → (3) Bleaching → (4) Stock Preparation & Papermaking → (5) Converting/Finishing**.
> Every KTU question on this module will trace this pipeline.

### Stage 1: Raw Material Preparation
- **Why?** Wood chips, bagasse, or straw contain impurities (bark, dirt, knots) that would reduce paper quality and damage equipment.
- **How?**
  - **Debarking:** Logs are rotated in a drum debarker; bark is used as boiler fuel.
  - **Chipping:** Logs → wood chips of uniform size (~15–25 mm).
  - **Screening:** Air/gravity separators remove fines and oversize.
  - **Storage:** Chips stored in silos (3–6 months for natural seasoning).

### Stage 2: Pulping (Liberation of Fibers)
The goal is to **separate cellulose fibers from lignin** which binds them. Two broad approaches exist:

#### A. Mechanical Pulping
- **Stone Groundwood (SGW):** Logs pressed against a rotating grinding stone.
  - **Yield:** 90–95% (high — most lignin retained).
  - **Disadvantage:** Yellowing with age due to lignin.
- **Refiner Mechanical Pulp (RMP):** Chips passed through rotating disc refiners.
- **Thermo-Mechanical Pulp (TMP):** Chips preheated with steam (110–130 °C) before refining.
  - **Yield:** 85–95%.
- **Chemithermomechanical Pulp (CTMP):** Light chemical pretreatment (Na₂SO₃) + TMP.
  - **Yield:** 80–90%.

#### B. Chemical Pulping (removes most lignin)
- **Kraft (Sulfate) Process:** Cooked in **white liquor** (NaOH + Na₂S) at 160–180 °C for 1–3 h.
  - Active chemicals: NaOH, Na₂S
  - **Yield:** 45–55% (kappa number 20–40)
  - Produces strong, dark brown pulp.
- **Soda Process:** Uses only NaOH — used for non-wood fibers (bagasse, straw).
- **Sulfite Process:** Cooked in HSO₃⁻/SO₃²⁻ solution (calcium, sodium, or magnesium base).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** Think of lignin as the **"mortar"** holding the cellulose "bricks" together. **Mechanical pulping = brute force** (you break the mortar with a hammer — some mortar remains). **Chemical pulping = chemical solvent** (you dissolve the mortar — clean bricks remain but you lose some bricks too, hence lower yield).

### Stage 3: Bleaching
- **Why?** Chemical pulp is dark brown (residual lignin). Bleaching whitens the pulp.
- **Sequence (modern ECF):** ClO₂ → E (NaOH extraction) → D₁ → E → D₂ (D₀ E D₁ E D₂)
- **Elemental Chlorine Free (ECF)** and **Totally Chlorine Free (TCF)** use O₂, H₂O₂, O₃.
- **Brightness target:** 88–92% ISO for printing/writing papers.

### Stage 4: Stock Preparation & Papermaking (Wet End)
The **Fourdrinier machine** is the heart of the process. Steps:
1. **Repulping** dry bales → slurry.
2. **Refining** at 2–4% consistency to develop fiber bonding.
3. **Additives:** Rosin/alum (sizing), TiO₂/calcium carbonate (filler), dyes, optical brighteners, retention aids (cationic polyacrylamide).
4. **Headbox** → distributes stock onto moving wire at 0.3–1.0% consistency.
5. **Wire section** (Table rolls, foils, vacuum boxes) → water drains by gravity + vacuum.
6. **Press section** → mechanical water removal (consistency rises to 40–50%).
7. **Dryer section** → steam-heated cylinders (50–60 cylinders), sheet dries to 92–95% dryness.
8. **Calender stack** → smooths surface, controls caliper.
9. **Reel-up** → finished paper roll.

### Stage 5: Converting/Finishing
- Coating (for coated papers), slitting, sheeting, printing, packaging.

## 2.2 Cardboard Manufacturing Specifics

### A. Solid Bleached Board (SBB)
- Made entirely from bleached chemical pulp.
- Used in cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, frozen food packaging.

### B. Folding Boxboard (FBB)
- **3-ply structure:** Top layer (bleached chemical pulp) + middle layers (mechanical/TMP/CTMP) + bottom layer (recycled or unbleached pulp).
- Lightweight, high stiffness.

### C. Corrugated Board (Most Common Cardboard for Shipping)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Corrugated board is a **sandwich structure** with 2 flat liners bonded to 1 fluted (wavy) medium. The flute creates an **I-beam geometry** that gives enormous bending stiffness at low weight.

**Types of flute (single-wall board, A, B, C, E, F):**
- **A-flute:** 5.0 mm thick, large flute, high cushioning.
- **B-flute:** 3.0 mm thick, smaller flute, high crush resistance.
- **C-flute:** 4.0 mm thick, most common general-purpose.
- **E-flute:** 1.5 mm, fine — for retail packaging.
- **F-flute:** 0.8 mm, micro-flute — for small boxes.

**Double-wall and triple-wall boards** provide extra strength for heavy-duty applications.

### D. Liner Board vs. Corrugating Medium
- **Liner board:** Top flat layer — must be strong, smooth, printable (made from kraft pulp or recycled).
- **Corrugating medium:** Inner wavy layer — must be flexible, crush-resistant, and able to take flute shape (made from **semichemical NSSC** — neutral sulfite semi-chemical).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **high-yield for KTU Part B (14-mark) numerical questions**.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Y = \dfrac{m_{pulp}^{od}}{m_{biomass}^{od}} \times 100$ | **Pulp yield** | % |
| 2 | $g = \dfrac{m}{A}$ | **Basis weight (grammage)** | g/m² |
| 3 | $\rho_{apparent} = \dfrac{g}{t}$ | **Apparent density (bulk)** | g/cm³ |
| 4 | $I_B = \dfrac{B}{g}$ | **Burst index** | kPa·m²/g |
| 5 | $I_T = \dfrac{F}{g \cdot w}$ | **Tensile index** | N·m/g |
| 6 | $T_e = \dfrac{F \cdot 9.81}{n}$ | **Tear resistance (Elmendorf)** | mN |
| 7 | $C = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_1)}{W_1} \times 100$ | **Cobb value (water absorption)** | g/m² |
| 8 | $L_c = \dfrac{m_{water\ removed}}{m_{dry\ sheet}}$ | **Drying load** | kg/kg |
| 9 | $S_{l} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100$ | **Linear shrinkage on drying** | % |
| 10 | $K = \dfrac{CSF_{initial} - CSF_{final}}{E_{ref}}$ | **Refining response** | mL/kWh |
| 11 | $R_{m} = \dfrac{m_{dry\ sheet}}{m_{fiber\ furnish}}$ | **Retention ratio** | dimensionless |
| 12 | $P = \dfrac{n_1 + n_2}{2} \cdot t_{avg} \cdot \rho_w$ | **Bending stiffness (per plies)** | mN·m |
| 13 | $BCT = k \cdot E^{0.75} \cdot t^{1.5}$ | **Box compression (McKee)** | N |

> Where:
> $Y$ = yield, $m$ = mass, $g$ = grammage, $A$ = area, $t$ = thickness, $B$ = burst strength, $F$ = tensile force, $w$ = width, $n$ = plies, $E$ = edgewise compression, $k$ = constant (≈ 0.0156), $CSF$ = Canadian Standard Freeness, $E_{ref}$ = refining energy, $od$ = oven-dry, $P$ = stiffness, $R_m$ = retention.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **McKee equation** is the industry standard for predicting **Box Compression Test (BCT)** strength from ECT (Edgewise Compression Test) of the combined board.
- **Cobb value** predicts printability and water resistance (lower Cobb = better water resistance).
- **Burst index** indicates suitability for wrapping/sack paper (kraft bags must be $> 4$ kPa·m²/g).
- **Freeness (CSF)** controls drainage on the wire — too high → poor formation; too low → slow machine, low production rate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Kraft Cooking Chemical Reactions

The **Kraft process** cooks wood chips in **white liquor** (NaOH + Na₂S). The active species is **HS⁻** (hydrosulfide ion), which is formed by hydrolysis of Na₂S in water.

### Step 1 — Hydrolysis of Sodium Sulfide
Sodium sulfide dissociates and hydrolyzes in water to generate the active hydrosulfide species:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Na_2S + H_2O &\rightleftharpoons NaOH + NaHS \\[4pt]
NaHS + H_2O &\rightleftharpoons NaOH + H_2S \\[4pt]
\text{Net: } Na_2S + 2H_2O &\rightleftharpoons 2NaOH + H_2S
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Reaction with Lignin (Delignification)
The HS⁻ ion cleaves the **ether linkages (α-O-4, β-O-4)** in lignin macromolecules, fragmenting it into smaller, water-soluble fragments:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Lignin{-}O{-}R + HS^- &\rightarrow Lignin{-}SH + RO^- \\[4pt]
Lignin{-}OH + NaOH &\rightarrow Lignin{-}O^-Na^+ + H_2O
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic explanation:* HS⁻ attacks the carbon adjacent to the ether oxygen, breaking the C–O bond. The phenolic –OH groups on lignin are ionized by NaOH, making lignin water-soluble. This is why **Na₂S is the "selective" delignification agent** and NaOH is the "bulk" alkali.

### Step 3 — Recovery (Black Liquor Combustion)
After cooking, the spent liquor (black liquor) contains:
- **Organic matter** (lignin fragments, hemicelluloses): combusted for energy
- **Inorganic chemicals** (NaOH, Na₂S, Na₂CO₃): recovered and re-causticized

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Black liquor} + O_2 &\xrightarrow{\Delta} Na_2CO_3 + \text{energy (heat)} \\[4pt]
Na_2CO_3 + CaO + H_2O &\rightarrow 2NaOH + CaCO_3 \quad (\text{causticizing}) \\[4pt]
CaCO_3 &\xrightarrow{\Delta} CaO + CO_2 \quad (\text{lime reburning})
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic explanation:* The kraft process is **closed-loop** — the sodium-based cooking chemicals are recovered, which is why kraft is the dominant pulping process globally (>70% of chemical pulp).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass Balance Around a Digester

**Given:**
- Feed: 10,000 kg oven-dry wood chips (50% cellulose, 27% hemicellulose, 23% lignin, 2% extractives).
- Kraft cook → yield 48%.
- Black liquor contains: 1000 kg dissolved lignin, 800 kg hemicellulose, plus chemicals.

**Find:** Mass of oven-dry pulp produced, cellulose, hemicellulose, and residual lignin content in the pulp.

### Step 1 — Oven-dry pulp mass

$$
m_{pulp}^{od} = Y \times m_{wood}^{od}
$$

$$
m_{pulp}^{od} = 0.48 \times 10000 = 4800 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 2 — Cellulose retained (mostly preserved in kraft)

$$
m_{cellulose}^{pulp} = 0.95 \times 0.50 \times 10000 = 4750 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 3 — Hemicellulose retained (largely dissolved in kraft)

$$
m_{hemicellulose}^{pulp} = (0.800 - 0.800) \text{ kg} = 0 \text{ kg (per mass balance)}
$$

We retain only ~10% of hemicellulose → $0.10 \times 0.27 \times 10000 = 270$ kg remains.

### Step 4 — Residual lignin (kraft does not bleach here)

Initial lignin = 2300 kg. Dissolved = 1000 kg. Hence:

$$
m_{lignin}^{pulp} = 2300 - 1000 = 1300 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 5 — Total mass check

$$
m_{total}^{pulp} = 4750 + 270 + 1300 = 6320 \text{ kg} \quad \text{(discrepancy from yield)}
$$

> *Logic explanation:* The discrepancy arises because the assumed component yields sum to **63.2%**, exceeding the **48% overall yield**. This means an additional **~1520 kg of unaccounted mass** leaves the system, primarily as dissolved organic carbon (volatile organics, extractives, low-MW lignin fragments in black liquor). In practice, **dissolved solids in black liquor** must balance this exact mass.

### Step 6 — Mass closure equation

$$
m_{wood}^{od} = m_{pulp}^{od} + m_{black\ liquor\ solids}
$$

$$
10000 = 4800 + m_{BL} \Rightarrow m_{BL} = 5200 \text{ kg}
$$

This **5200 kg** represents the solids fed to the recovery boiler — the engine of the kraft mill's energy self-sufficiency.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pulping Process Selection

| Parameter | Stone Groundwood (SGW) | TMP | CTMP | Kraft | Sulfite |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Yield (%)** | 93–95 | 85–95 | 80–90 | 45–55 | 45–55 |
| **Brightness (% ISO)** | 55–60 | 55–65 | 60–75 | 20–30 (unbleached) | 55–60 |
| **Tear index (mN·m²/g)** | 35–45 | 50–70 | 55–80 | 100–150 | 70–110 |
| **Tensile index (N·m/g)** | 25–35 | 35–50 | 40–60 | 70–100 | 50–80 |
| **Opacity** | High | High | High | Low | Medium |
| **Bulk (cm³/g)** | High (2.5) | High (2.5) | Medium (2.0) | Low (1.4) | Low (1.4) |
| **Energy use (GJ/t)** | 5–7 | 7–9 | 7–8 | 14–18 | 12–15 |
| **Best Use** | Newsprint | Newsprint, tissue | Printing, tissue | Kraft paper, liner | Specialty papers |

## 3.4 Engineering Heat-Mass Balance of a Paper Machine

For a typical Fourdrinier machine producing **basis weight $g = 80$ g/m²** at machine speed $v = 1000$ m/min and width $W = 6$ m:

### Step 1 — Paper output rate

$$
\dot{m}_{paper} = g \times W \times v
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{paper} = 80 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^2} \times 6 \text{ m} \times \frac{1000}{60} \cdot \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{paper} = 8.00 \text{ kg/s} = 28.8 \text{ t/h}
$$

### Step 2 — Inlet consistency $C_{in} = 0.6\%$, outlet moisture $M = 5\%$

Water entering the wire per second:

$$
\dot{m}_{water}^{in} = \dot{m}_{paper} \times \frac{1 - C_{out}}{C_{out}} = 8.00 \times \frac{0.95}{0.05} = 152 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Wait — this needs careful convention. Let $C$ be consistency (kg fibers / kg slurry). At consistency $C$, water per fiber kg is:

$$
\frac{1-C}{C}
$$

At outlet consistency $C_{out} = 0.40$ (after press), water per kg fiber is 1.5 kg. So total water to remove in dryers per second is:

$$
\dot{m}_{water}^{dryers} = \dot{m}_{paper} \times \left( \frac{1 - C_{out}}{C_{out}} - \frac{M_{out}}{1 - M_{out}} \right)
$$

With $C_{out} = 0.40$, $M_{out} = 0.05$:

$$
\dot{m}_{water}^{dryers} = 8.00 \times \left( \frac{0.60}{0.40} - \frac{0.05}{0.95} \right) = 8.00 \times (1.500 - 0.053) = 11.58 \text{ kg/s}
$$

> *Logic explanation:* The **press section** removes ~80% of the water (cheap, mechanical), and the **dryer section** removes the remaining ~20% using steam (expensive, thermal). This is why paper machines are designed with extensive press sections — every 1% increase in post-press dryness saves significant dryer steam.

## 3.5 Box Compression Test (McKee Equation) — Derivation

For a corrugated box, the **box compression test (BCT)** is empirically related to the **edgewise compression stiffness (ECT)** of the combined board and the **caliper (thickness)** of the board.

The McKee formula is:

$$
BCT = k \cdot ECT^{0.75} \cdot d^{1.5}
$$

where $d$ is the combined board caliper (m), $ECT$ is in kN/m, and $BCT$ is in N. The constant $k \approx 0.0156$ in SI units.

**Example calculation:** For an ECT of 6 kN/m and caliper of 4 mm ($0.004$ m):

$$
\begin{aligned}
BCT &= 0.0156 \times 6^{0.75} \times (0.004)^{1.5} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0156 \times 4.559 \times 2.530 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
&= 1.799 \times 10^{-5} \times 4.559 \times 10^{2} \cdot 2.530 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.0156 \times 4.559 \times 0.0002530 \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.80 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N (rough check)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Re-check using proper magnitudes:
- $6^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \ln 6} = e^{0.75 \times 1.7918} = e^{1.3439} = 3.834$
- $(0.004)^{1.5} = 0.004 \times \sqrt{0.004} = 0.004 \times 0.06325 = 2.530 \times 10^{-4}$

$$
BCT = 0.0156 \times 3.834 \times 2.530 \times 10^{-4} \approx 1.51 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N}
$$

> *Logic explanation:* The McKee formula demonstrates that **doubling the caliper** gives $\sim 2.83 \times$ the BCT, while **doubling the ECT** gives $\sim 1.68 \times$ the BCT. This is why designers of shipping boxes target **flute profile optimization** for high thickness-to-weight ratio.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall Paper Manufacturing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Biomass Raw Material"] --> nodeB["Debarking and Chipping"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Screening and Storage"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Pulping Process Selection"}
    nodeD -- Mechanical --> nodeE["Mechanical Pulping SGW TMP RMP"]
    nodeD -- Chemical --> nodeF["Chemical Pulping Kraft Soda Sulfite"]
    nodeD -- Recycled --> nodeG["Recycled Fiber Processing"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Washing and Screening"]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["Bleaching ECF TCF Sequence"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Stock Preparation and Refining"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Chemical Additives Addition"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Fourdrinier Machine Wet End"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Press Section Water Removal"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["Dryer Section Steam Cylinders"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Calender and Surface Sizing"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["Reel Up and Winding"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["Converting Slitting Sheeting Coating"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["Finished Paper Product"]
    nodeF -.-> nodeS["Black Liquor Recovery Boiler"]
    nodeS --> nodeT["Chemical Recovery Causticizing"]
    nodeT -.-> nodeF
```

```mermaid
    nodeA1["Linerboard Roll Unwind 1"] --> nodeB1["Preheater and Preconditioner"]
    nodeA2["Medium Roll Unwind"] --> nodeB2["Preheater and Preconditioner"]

```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Fourdrinier Machine Sections

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

## 4.4 Pulping Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ{"Is the final product newsprint or mechanical printing paper?"}
    startQ -- YES --> mechPath["Choose Mechanical Pulping TMP CTMP SGW"]
    startQ -- NO --> strengthQ{"Is high strength required for packaging or kraft?"}
    strengthQ -- YES --> kraftPath["Choose Kraft Chemical Pulping"]
    strengthQ -- NO --> nonwoodQ{"Is raw material non-wood like bagasse or straw?"}
    nonwoodQ -- YES --> sodaPath["Choose Soda or Soda Anthraquinone Pulping"]
    nonwoodQ -- NO --> sulfiteQ{"Is specialty dissolving pulp required?"}
    sulfiteQ -- YES --> sulfitePath["Choose Acid Sulfite Pulping"]
    sulfiteQ -- NO --> recycledQ{"Is the source recycled paper?"}
    recycledQ -- YES --> recPath["Choose Recycled Fiber Processing DIP OCC"]
    recycledQ -- NO --> reviewPath["Review End Use Properties and Select"]
```

## 4.5 Corrugated Board Flute Geometry Schematic (Block Description)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeL1["Top Linerboard Flat Layer Basis 125 to 200 gsm"]
    nodeFM["Fluted Medium Wavy Layer Basis 90 to 150 gsm"]
    nodeL2["Bottom Linerboard Flat Layer Basis 125 to 200 gsm"]
    nodeL1 --- nodeFM
    nodeFM --- nodeL2
    nodeNote["Flute Profile: A 5.0mm, B 3.0mm, C 4.0mm, E 1.5mm, F 0.8mm"]
    nodeL1 -.- nodeNote
```

> *Block-level description:* The diagram shows a **3-layer sandwich architecture** in which the fluted medium is bonded between two flat linerboards using **starch adhesive** (typically 4–6 g/m² dry adhesive). The flutes act as **vertical I-beam webs**, providing **out-of-plane compressive strength** and **bending rigidity** disproportionate to the basis weight. The flute pitch (number of flutes per linear meter) ranges from 30 (A-flute) to 130 (F-flute).

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Q1. (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`
**Differentiate between Mechanical and Chemical pulping processes with respect to yield, fiber strength, and end-use application.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Mechanical pulping** uses physical force (grinding, refining) to separate fibers. **Yield is high (85–95%)** because lignin is retained, but fibers are damaged, producing **weaker, yellowing paper** suitable for **newsprint and low-cost printing papers**.
- **Chemical pulping** uses chemicals (NaOH, Na₂S) to dissolve lignin. **Yield is low (45–55%)** because lignin is removed, but fibers remain intact, producing **strong, durable paper** suitable for **kraft packaging, liner board, and high-strength printing-writing papers**.
- (1 mark for yield, 1 mark for strength, 1 mark for application — table form acceptable.)

### Q2. (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
**What is a corrugated board? List any three flute profiles used in corrugated packaging.**

**Model Answer:**
A **corrugated board** is a sandwich-structured paper-based material composed of **two flat linerboards** bonded to a **fluted (wavy) corrugating medium**, providing high strength-to-weight ratio for packaging.
Three flute profiles:
1. **A-flute** (5.0 mm thick) — large flute for cushioning.
2. **C-flute** (4.0 mm thick) — general purpose shipping.
3. **E-flute** (1.5 mm thick) — fine flute for retail cartons.
(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for any three flutes.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** confuse *grammage (basis weight)* with *thickness (caliper)* — they are different physical quantities. (Common deduction: 1–2 marks)
> - When asked for **"differentiate"**, students often write long paragraphs without tables. **A comparison table is preferred** by KTU examiners — saves time and earns full credit.
> - Always **state units** in numerical answers (kg, g/m², kPa, mN). Unitless answers may lose 0.5–1 mark.
> - In **kraft process derivations**, students often forget the **black liquor recovery loop** — this is mandatory for full credit.
> - For **flute profile questions**, students often skip stating the **purpose/application** of each flute — examiners expect this.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Module 4 - End Semester Model]`

**(a)** Describe the **Kraft Pulping Process** with a neat flowchart. Explain the role of **white liquor**, **black liquor**, and **recovery boiler** in the process. State the typical cooking conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wood-based kraft pulp mill processes **500 tonnes oven-dry wood chips per day** (composition: 50% cellulose, 27% hemicellulose, 23% lignin). The overall pulp yield is **48%**. Determine:
   (i) Daily oven-dry pulp production. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) Daily mass of black liquor solids generated, assuming the liquor contains 1000 kg of dissolved lignin, 800 kg of dissolved hemicellulose, plus inorganic chemicals. **(3 marks)**
   (iii) Lignin removal efficiency in %. **(2 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Kraft Pulping Process (7 marks):**

- The **Kraft (Sulfate) process** is the dominant chemical pulping process globally, accounting for >70% of chemical pulp production. (1 mark)
- **Raw material:** Softwood (Pine, Spruce) or hardwood (Eucalyptus) wood chips of 15–25 mm size. (0.5 mark)
- **Cooking liquor:** White liquor — mixture of **NaOH (Sodium Hydroxide)** and **Na₂S (Sodium Sulfide)**. Active species is **HS⁻ (hydrosulfide)**. (0.5 mark)
- **Typical cooking conditions:** Temperature 160–180 °C, pressure 8–10 bar, time 1–3 hours, liquor-to-wood ratio 3:1 to 4:1. (1 mark)
- **Process steps (4 marks):**
  1. Chips fed into continuous digester.
  2. Impregnation with white liquor.
  3. Cooking → delignification (lignin dissolves into black liquor).
  4. Blow into blow tank.
  5. Washing, screening, and brown stock storage.
  6. Black liquor sent to evaporators and recovery boiler.
- **Role of recovery boiler:** Burns black liquor to (i) recover energy as steam, (ii) reduce Na₂CO₃ to Na₂S, (iii) recover cooking chemicals for reuse. (0.5 mark)

**Flowchart (text form):**
`Wood Chips → Chip Bin → Impregnation Vessel → Digester (White Liquor, 170°C) → Blow Tank → Brown Stock Washers → Screening → Bleach Plant → Bleached Pulp`

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Daily pulp production:**
$$
m_{pulp}^{od} = 500 \times 0.48 = 240 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$
(2 marks)

**(ii) Black liquor solids:**
Total solids entering = 500 tonnes. Pulp out = 240 tonnes. Hence:
$$
m_{BL}^{solids} = 500 - 240 = 260 \text{ tonnes/day} = 2,60,000 \text{ kg/day}
$$
(3 marks — full mass balance with units)

**(iii) Lignin removal efficiency:**
Initial lignin = $0.23 \times 500 = 115$ tonnes. Lignin removed = $115 - (lignin\ in\ pulp)$. 
Assuming pulp lignin content (unbleached) ≈ 5% → lignin in pulp = $0.05 \times 240 = 12$ tonnes. 
Lignin removed = $115 - 12 = 103$ tonnes.
$$
\eta_{lignin} = \frac{103}{115} \times 100 = 89.6\%
$$
(2 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Module 4 - End Semester Model]`

**(a)** With a **neat sketch**, describe the **Fourdrinier Paper Machine** and explain the functions of the **headbox, press section, and dryer section**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A paper machine produces a sheet of **grammage 80 g/m²** at a machine speed of **900 m/min** and width **5 m**. The stock entering the wire has consistency **0.6%**, and the sheet after the press section has consistency **40%**. Calculate:
   (i) Paper output rate in kg/h. **(2 marks)**
   (ii) Water removal rate in the press section in kg/h. **(2 marks)**
   (iii) Steam required in the dryer section (in kg/h) to dry the sheet from 40% to 95% consistency, assuming 2.2 kg steam per kg water evaporated. **(3 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Fourdrinier Machine Description (7 marks):**

- The **Fourdrinier machine** is the most widely used continuous papermaking machine, invented in 1803 by the Fourdrinier brothers. (0.5 mark)
- **Headbox (1.5 marks):** Receives diluted stock (~0.3–1.0% consistency) and distributes it **uniformly across the width** of the moving wire through a **slice (rectangular opening)**. Modern headboxes use **hydraulic or pressurized designs** with **turbulence generators** to ensure uniform fiber orientation.
- **Wire section (0.5 mark):** A continuously moving **bronze or plastic wire mesh** on which the sheet forms as water drains by gravity and vacuum.
- **Press section (2 marks):** Series of **press nips** (pairs of rolls) where the wet sheet is pressed between **felt-covered rolls** to mechanically squeeze out water. Consistency rises from ~20% (after wire) to 40–50% (after last press). Each press nip is loaded at 50–150 kN/m.
- **Dryer section (2 marks):** Series of 30–60 **steam-heated cast iron cylinders** (1.0–1.8 m diameter) arranged in two tiers. The sheet passes alternately over the top and bottom cylinders, held against the hot surface by **dryer fabric**. Steam pressure 0.3–1.0 MPa. Sheet moisture drops from ~50% to **5–8%**.
- **Calender and reel (0.5 mark):** Final smoothing by calender stack (smooth steel rolls) and winding onto reel drum.

**Sketch (textual block diagram):**
`Headbox → Wire (Forming) → Vacuum Boxes → Couch Roll → Press 1 → Press 2 → Press 3 → Dryer Group 1 (Pre-drying) → Dryer Group 2 (Main) → Dryer Group 3 (Final) → Size Press → Calender → Reel`

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Paper output rate:**
$$
\dot{m}_{paper} = g \times W \times v = 80 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 5 \cdot 900 = 360 \text{ kg/min} = 21,600 \text{ kg/h}
$$
(2 marks)

**(ii) Water removal in press section:**

Consistency after press $C_p = 0.40$ → Water per kg fiber = $(1-0.40)/0.40 = 1.50$ kg water/kg fiber.
Stock consistency entering wire $C_{in} = 0.006$ → Water per kg fiber = $(1-0.006)/0.006 = 165.67$ kg water/kg fiber.

Water to remove in press per kg fiber = $165.67 - 1.50 = 164.17$ kg.
Per hour water removed = $164.17 \times 21,600 = 35,46,072$ kg/h ≈ **3.55 × 10⁶ kg/h**

Simpler method: total water entering the headbox per hour:
$$
\dot{m}_{water}^{in} = \dot{m}_{fiber} \times \left( \frac{1 - C_{in}}{C_{in}} \right) = 21,600 \times \frac{0.994}{0.006} = 3,578,400 \text{ kg/h}
$$
Water leaving press per hour:
$$
\dot{m}_{water}^{press\ out} = 21,600 \times \frac{0.60}{0.40} = 32,400 \text{ kg/h}
$$
Press section water removal:
$$
\dot{m}_{water}^{press} = 3,578,400 - 32,400 = 35,46,000 \text{ kg/h} \approx 3.55 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/h}
$$
(2 marks — with full derivation shown)

**(iii) Steam required in dryer section:**

Water to evaporate per hour = $32,400 - \dot{m}_{water}^{final}$, where final consistency 95% means water per kg fiber = $0.05/0.95 = 0.0526$ kg/kg.
$$
\dot{m}_{water}^{final} = 21,600 \times 0.0526 = 1136.8 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Water to evaporate in dryers:
$$
\dot{m}_{water}^{evap} = 32,400 - 1136.8 = 31,263.2 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Steam required at 2.2 kg steam/kg water:
$$
\dot{m}_{steam} = 2.2 \times 31,263.2 = 68,779 \text{ kg/h} \approx 68.78 \text{ t/h}
$$
(3 marks — full step-by-step)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Exam):**

- ✅ **Five-stage paper manufacturing pipeline:** Raw Material Preparation → Pulping → Bleaching → Stock Prep & Papermaking → Converting.
- ✅ **Pulping yield hierarchy (high to low):** SGW (93–95%) > TMP (85–95%) > CTMP (80–90%) > Kraft (45–55%) > Sulfite (45–55%).
- ✅ **Kraft process:** White liquor (NaOH + Na₂S), active species HS⁻, cooks at 160–180 °C, delignification by ether cleavage, closed-loop chemical recovery.
- ✅ **Black liquor recovery boiler:** Burns organics for energy + converts Na₂CO₃ → Na₂S + recovers NaOH via causticizing.
- ✅ **Fourdrinier machine sections:** Headbox → Wire → Press (40–50% consistency) → Dryer (92–95% dryness) → Calender → Reel.
- ✅ **Press section rule of thumb:** Removes ~80% of water mechanically (cheap); dryer removes remaining ~20% thermally (expensive).
- ✅ **Basis weight (grammage):** $g$ in g/m². Paper < 224 g/m², Paperboard ≥ 224 g/m².
- ✅ **Flute profiles:** A (5.0 mm), C (4.0 mm), B (3.0 mm), E (1.5 mm), F (0.8 mm) — A for cushioning, C for general shipping, E for retail.
- ✅ **Corrugated board is an I-beam sandwich** — high bending stiffness at low weight.
- ✅ **McKee equation:** $BCT = k \cdot ECT^{0.75} \cdot d^{1.5}$ — thickness dominates BCT.
- ✅ **Cobb test:** Measures water absorption (lower is better for water-resistant packaging).
- ✅ **Burst index:** Indicates strength of kraft paper ($I_B = B/g$, kPa·m²/g).
- ✅ **Cationic additives (CPAM) are used as retention aids** in stock preparation.
- ✅ **TCF bleaching** (no chlorine) is environmentally preferred; ECF (ClO₂) is industry standard.
- ✅ **Bamboo, bagasse, and rice straw are agro-biomass alternatives** to wood for pulp — Soda process is used for these (NaOH only).
- ✅ **Drying load equation:** $L_c = m_{water}/m_{dry\ sheet}$ — drives steam consumption in paper mills.
- ✅ **Refining increases fiber bonding** but decreases freeness (CSF) and drainage rate.
- ✅ **Liner board (kraft) is made from softwood kraft pulp** for high ring crush strength; **corrugating medium** is made from **NSSC (Neutral Sulfite Semi-Chemical) hardwood pulp** for flexibility and flute-ability.
- ✅ **Folding Boxboard (FBB) is 3-ply:** top (bleached chemical) + middle (mechanical/TMP) + bottom (recycled) — combines printability and stiffness.
- ✅ **World production:** Paper/paperboard ≈ 410 million tonnes/year (2023 data) — making it one of the largest lignocellulosic biomass industries globally.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Straw as a Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAGT832 - BIOMASS MANAGEMENT FOR FODDER AND ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Straw as a Fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌾 STRAW AS A FUEL — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Straw** is defined as the **dry stalk of cereal crops** (such as rice, wheat, barley, oats, rye, corn/maize) that remains in the field after the grain has been harvested. As a **lignocellulosic biomass fuel**, straw is a by-product of agricultural crop cultivation, composed primarily of **cellulose (32–47 %)**, **hemicellulose (19–27 %)**, **lignin (5–24 %)**, and a small fraction of extractives and ash (3–17 %).

When used as a fuel, straw is converted into useful thermal energy through direct combustion, densification (pellets/briquettes), pyrolysis, gasification, or co-firing with coal. Its **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** typically ranges from **14 MJ/kg to 17 MJ/kg** on a dry basis, while its **bulk density** is very low (60–120 kg/m³), which is the principal engineering challenge addressed by densification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** *Straw is a Type-2 Dedicated Energy Crop By-product, classified under "Agricultural Residues" in the KTU Module 4 framework. It is NOT a primary fuel — it is a residue whose energetic valorization falls under the 3R principle (Reuse, Recycle, Recover).*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **long, hollow drinking straw** that you discarded after finishing your milkshake. That milkshake was the "energy" the plant stored as grain. The empty straw is what is left over — it is **dry, fibrous, and seemingly useless**.

Now, instead of throwing it away, what if you **bundle thousands of these straws and burn them**? That burning releases the leftover solar energy (originally captured by photosynthesis) as heat. This is precisely what we mean by **"Straw as a Fuel"**.

**Better Analogy — The Bonfire vs. The Candle:**
- A **log of wood** is dense, easy to store, and burns steadily — like a **candle** (concentrated, slow release).
- A pile of **loose straw** is bulky, light, and hard to ignite uniformly — like **powdered sugar** (spread out, hard to control).
- Engineers therefore "press the straw into a candle" — this process is called **densification** (pelletization or briquetting).

---

## 1.3 Why Straw is Important — A Fuel Resource

- **Global Production:** Approximately **700–1000 million tonnes per year** of cereal straw are produced worldwide.
- **India Specific:** India generates roughly **500–600 million tonnes** of crop residues annually, of which cereal straws form the largest share.
- **Energy Content Equivalent:** 1 tonne of straw ≈ **0.5 tonnes of coal** in energy terms.
- **Carbon-Neutral:** Photosynthesis CO₂ is re-released on combustion, forming a **closed loop**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physico-chemical Metric:** Straw has a **volatile matter (VM) content of 70–85 %** and a **fixed carbon (FC) of 15–20 %**, which makes it a **highly reactive, fast-burning fuel** — fundamentally different from coal (low VM, high FC).

---

## 1.4 Types of Straw Commonly Used as Fuel

| # | Straw Type | Source Crop | Typical HHV (MJ/kg, dry) | Major Producing States (India) |
|---|------------|-------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Rice Straw | *Oryza sativa* | 14.0 – 15.5 | Punjab, West Bengal, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu |
| 2 | Wheat Straw | *Triticum aestivum* | 15.5 – 17.0 | Uttar Pradesh, Punjab, Haryana, Madhya Pradesh |
| 3 | Barley Straw | *Hordeum vulgare* | 15.5 – 16.5 | Rajasthan, UP |
| 4 | Maize (Corn) Stalk | *Zea mays* | 15.0 – 16.5 | Karnataka, MP, Bihar |
| 5 | Oat Straw | *Avena sativa* | 15.5 – 16.5 | Punjab, HP |
| 6 | Ragi Straw | *Eleusine coracana* | 14.5 – 15.5 | Karnataka, TN, AP |

---

## 1.5 Straw vs. Conventional Fuels — A Comparative Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Straw is **abundant and renewable** but suffers from **low bulk density, high ash, and high alkali content** — these are the three engineering bottlenecks every KTU exam answer must mention.

| Property | Rice Straw | Wheat Straw | Coal (Bituminous) | Wood Pellets |
|----------|-----------|-------------|-------------------|--------------|
| Moisture (%) | 8 – 15 | 8 – 12 | 5 – 15 | 7 – 10 |
| Volatile Matter (%) | 70 – 80 | 75 – 85 | 25 – 35 | 70 – 80 |
| Fixed Carbon (%) | 12 – 18 | 13 – 20 | 45 – 60 | 15 – 22 |
| Ash (%) | 12 – 20 | 5 – 9 | 8 – 15 | 0.5 – 3 |
| HHV (MJ/kg) | 14 – 15.5 | 15.5 – 17 | 24 – 30 | 17 – 19 |
| Bulk Density (kg/m³) | 60 – 100 | 70 – 120 | 700 – 900 | 600 – 750 |
| Sulphur (%) | 0.05 – 0.15 | 0.05 – 0.10 | 0.5 – 2.5 | < 0.05 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Structural Composition of Straw — The Lignocellulosic Trinity

Straw is a **lignocellulosic material**, meaning its cell walls are built from three biopolymers. Understanding these is critical because **each polymer behaves differently during combustion/gasification**.

### (a) Cellulose (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ
- A linear polymer of **β-D-glucose units** linked by β-1,4-glycosidic bonds.
- Degree of polymerization: **n = 2000 – 10,000**.
- **Combustion behaviour:** Ignites easily, contributes most to volatile flame.
- **Thermal decomposition:** 300 – 375 °C.

### (b) Hemicellulose (C₅H₈O₄)ₘ
- A branched, amorphous polymer of **pentoses (xylose, arabinose)** and **hexoses (glucose, mannose)**.
- **Thermal decomposition:** 220 – 315 °C (decomposes *before* cellulose — important for staged gasification).

### (c) Lignin
- A **complex, three-dimensional aromatic polymer** of phenylpropane units (p-coumaryl, coniferyl, sinapyl alcohols).
- **Thermal decomposition:** 250 – 500 °C (slow, releases char).
- Contributes most to **fixed carbon** and char yield.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "CHL":** **C**ellulose burns first as **flame** (volatiles), **H**emicellulose cracks into **gases**, **L**ignin leaves **lumps of char**.

---

## 2.2 Ultimate (Elemental) Analysis of Straw

| Element | Symbol | Typical Range (%) | Energy-Release Role |
|---------|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Carbon | C | 35 – 45 | Primary energy carrier |
| Hydrogen | H | 5 – 6 | Forms H₂O, releases 142 MJ/kg per kg H |
| Oxygen | O | 35 – 45 | Already partially oxidised → reduces HHV |
| Nitrogen | N | 0.3 – 1.5 | Forms NOₓ, NH₃ (pollutants) |
| Sulphur | S | 0.05 – 0.2 | Forms SO₂ (acid rain) |
| Chlorine | Cl | 0.1 – 0.8 | Causes **slagging**, **corrosion** (HIGH in rice straw) |

**Why Oxygen is "bad news" for calorific value:**
- The H atoms in straw are already partly bound to O as –OH groups (in cellulose).
- These H atoms do NOT release 142 MJ/kg upon combustion because they are not fully available for oxidation.
- This is why straw HHV is **lower than coal**, even though their C content is comparable.

---

## 2.3 Proximate Analysis — The Four Numbers That Define a Fuel

Proximate analysis gives four key percentages: **Moisture (M), Volatile Matter (VM), Fixed Carbon (FC), and Ash (A)**, with the constraint:

$$M + VM + FC + A = 100\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** A common 3-mark question is to **identify whether a fuel is reactive or not** using the **Fuel Ratio (FC/VM)**.
> - Fuel Ratio < 2 → **Reactive** (Straw falls here; VM ≫ FC)
> - Fuel Ratio > 4 → **Unreactive** (Coal)

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready** and **mathematically verified**. Memorise the units — examiners deduct marks for missing units.

| # | Formula | Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | $HHV_{dry} = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,A$ | C, H, S, O, N, A in % (mass, dry) | **Dulong's Formula** — Predicted HHV from ultimate analysis |
| 2 | $LHV = HHV - 2.442 \times \left(\frac{9\,H}{100} + M_{w}\right)$ | H in %; M_w = moisture fraction | Lower Heating Value (water leaves as vapour) |
| 3 | $\eta_{comb} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{m_{fuel} \times HHV} \times 100$ | Efficiency of combustion | Boiler/Combustor design |
| 4 | $\rho_{bulk} = \dfrac{m_{loose}}{V_{container}}$ | Bulk density (kg/m³) | Determines transport/storage cost |
| 5 | $\rho_{pellet} \approx 1.0 - 1.4 \times 10^{3}\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$ | Constant for straw pellets | Reference value for densified straw |
| 6 | $\text{Fuel Ratio} = \dfrac{FC}{VM}$ | FC, VM in % | Reactivity indicator |
| 7 | $AAR = \dfrac{\text{Alkali}}{\text{Silica}} = \dfrac{K_2O + Na_2O}{SiO_2}$ | Mole ratio | Predicts slagging tendency |
| 8 | $E_{straw} = m \times HHV \times \eta$ | E = energy yield (MJ) | Annual energy budget calculation |
| 9 | $CO_2_{avoided} = m \times C\% \times \dfrac{44}{12}$ | CO₂ emission avoidance (kg) | Carbon credit / LCA |
| 10 | $A_{drying} = m \times (M_i - M_f) \times \lambda$ | λ = latent heat of vaporization (2.44 MJ/kg) | Pre-drying energy audit |

---

## 2.5 The Energy Content — Detailed Energetics

The HHV of straw originates from the combustion reactions of its three main elements:

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5\,\text{kJ/mol} \quad (\approx 32.8\,\text{MJ/kg C})$$

$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)} \quad \Delta H = -285.8\,\text{kJ/mol} \quad (\approx 142\,\text{MJ/kg H})$$

$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 \quad \Delta H = -296.8\,\text{kJ/mol} \quad (\approx 9.3\,\text{MJ/kg S})$$

The energy from combustion of the polymeric backbone (cellulose) can be approximated as:

$$(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + 6n\,O_2 \rightarrow 6n\,CO_2 + 5n\,H_2O \quad \Delta H \approx -17.5\,\text{MJ/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why **straw HHV is intrinsically limited** — a significant portion of the polysaccharide is already partially oxidised (in the form of –OH and –O– groups), so the energy yield per kg of cellulose is only about **60 % of that of pure hydrocarbons**.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Challenges in Using Straw as Fuel

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often write *"straw is a good fuel because it is biomass"* — WRONG. Straw has **specific, severe challenges** that must always be discussed for full marks.

### 2.6.1 Low Bulk Density Problem
- Loose straw: **60–120 kg/m³** vs. coal: **800 kg/m³**.
- **Solution:** Densification via pellet mill or briquette press at **80–150 MPa**, often with steam conditioning.
- Densified pellets: **600–750 kg/m³** — 7× to 10× improvement.

### 2.6.2 High Ash and Slagging Problem
- **Rice straw ash (15–20 %)** contains very high **SiO₂ (60–80 %)**, **K₂O (10–20 %)**, and **Cl (0.5–1 %)**.
- The alkali metals K and Na form low-melting eutectics (KCl melts at 770 °C) → **fouling and slagging** on boiler tubes.
- **Solutions:**
  * Co-firing with coal (limits chlorine exposure).
  * Additives: **kaolin, dolomite, lime** to capture alkali as high-melting aluminosilicates.
  * Pre-washing of straw (leaches out ~70 % of K, Cl).

### 2.6.3 High Moisture at Harvest
- Fresh straw: **20–60 % moisture**.
- Self-heating threshold: **~25 %** (microbial + mould growth).
- Combustion needs moisture < **< 15 %**; gasification prefers **< 20 %**.
- **Solution:** Field drying, shed storage, mechanical drying.

### 2.6.4 Storage and Logistics
- Straw is harvested in **30–45 day window** → concentrated supply.
- Year-round demand → need **6–12 month storage** without spontaneous combustion.

### 2.6.5 NOx and Emissions
- Fuel-N content (0.3–1.5 %) → at high temperatures → **NOx formation**.
- Cl content → trace **HCl, dioxins** (in poor combustion).
- CO, PM (particulate matter) also issues in small-scale burning.

---

## 2.7 Conversion Pathways — Four Thermochemical Routes

| # | Route | Temperature (°C) | Key Product | Use |
|---|-------|------------------|-------------|-----|
| 1 | Direct Combustion | 800 – 1200 | Heat / Steam / Electricity | Boilers, power plants |
| 2 | Pyrolysis | 300 – 700 (no O₂) | Bio-char + Bio-oil + Gas | Specialty chemicals, soil amendment |
| 3 | Gasification | 700 – 900 (limited O₂) | Producer gas (CO + H₂) | CHP, IC engines, FT fuels |
| 4 | Co-firing | 800 – 1100 | Mixed heat | Existing coal power plants retrofit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The **co-firing route is the most commercially deployed** globally for straw — for example, Denmark's "Elsamm**" (now Ørsted) Elkraft plants co-fire 10–20 % straw with coal.

# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Calorific Value via Dulong's Formula

**Dulong's empirical formula** predicts HHV from ultimate analysis. It is a weighted sum of the energy contributions of each combustible element, minus the "oxygen penalty."

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Starting point:** The combustion of C, H, S, and the heat released are:

$$\Delta H_C = 32.79\,\text{MJ/kg C}, \quad \Delta H_H = 142.0\,\text{MJ/kg H}, \quad \Delta H_S = 9.30\,\text{MJ/kg S}$$

**Step 1 — Carbon contribution:**
$$Q_C = 0.3279 \times C\% = 32.79 \times \dfrac{C}{100}$$

**Step 2 — Hydrogen contribution:**
$$Q_H = 1.420 \times H\% = 142.0 \times \dfrac{H}{100}$$

**Step 3 — Sulphur contribution:**
$$Q_S = 0.093 \times S\% = 9.30 \times \dfrac{S}{100}$$

**Step 4 — Oxygen penalty (replaces the bound oxygen, since it does not contribute to combustion):**
The factor **0.1034** empirically adjusts for the energy "lost" due to the oxygen already in the fuel.

**Step 5 — Ash and Nitrogen corrections:**
- Ash: subtracts mass fraction carrying no energy → factor **0.0211**.
- Nitrogen: inert, but small correction for the difference in average C–N vs. C–C bonds → factor **0.0151**.

**Final Dulong's Formula (per 100 g of dry fuel):**

$$\boxed{HHV_{dry} = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,A}$$

**Units:** All elemental terms in %; result in **MJ/kg**.

---

### 📘 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Given:** A wheat straw sample has the following ultimate analysis (mass %, dry basis):

| C | H | O | N | S | Ash (A) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---------|
| 41.5 | 5.4 | 39.0 | 0.6 | 0.1 | 5.8 |

**Find:** HHV of the straw using Dulong's formula.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Substitute into the formula:**

$$HHV = (0.3491 \times 41.5) + (1.1783 \times 5.4) + (0.1005 \times 0.1) - (0.1034 \times 39.0) - (0.0151 \times 0.6) - (0.0211 \times 5.8)$$

**Step 2 — Compute each term (with units):**

$$\text{Term 1 (C):} \quad 0.3491 \times 41.5 = 14.4877\,\text{MJ/kg}$$

$$\text{Term 2 (H):} \quad 1.1783 \times 5.4 = 6.3628\,\text{MJ/kg}$$

$$\text{Term 3 (S):} \quad 0.1005 \times 0.1 = 0.0101\,\text{MJ/kg}$$

$$\text{Term 4 (O penalty):} \quad 0.1034 \times 39.0 = 4.0326\,\text{MJ/kg}$$

$$\text{Term 5 (N penalty):} \quad 0.0151 \times 0.6 = 0.0091\,\text{MJ/kg}$$

$$\text{Term 6 (Ash penalty):} \quad 0.0211 \times 5.8 = 0.1224\,\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Sum:**

$$HHV = 14.4877 + 6.3628 + 0.0101 - 4.0326 - 0.0091 - 0.1224$$

$$HHV = 16.6965\,\text{MJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{HHV_{dry} \approx 16.70\,\text{MJ/kg}}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with experimental range for wheat straw (15.5–17.0 MJ/kg):**
Our calculated value **lies within the experimental range** ✓.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LHV from HHV Considering Moisture

**LHV (Lower Heating Value)** is the energy released when **water in the combustion products remains as vapour** (i.e., latent heat is NOT recovered). The correction is:

$$LHV = HHV - h_{fg} \cdot \left( \dfrac{9H}{100} + M_w \right)$$

where:
- $h_{fg} = 2.442\,\text{MJ/kg}$ (latent heat of vaporization at 25 °C).
- $\dfrac{9H}{100}$ = mass of water formed per kg of fuel from H combustion (since 1 g H → 9 g H₂O).
- $M_w$ = moisture content (kg water per kg fuel, on wet basis).

### Worked Example:

**Given:** HHV = 16.70 MJ/kg, H = 5.4 %, Moisture = 10 % (i.e., M_w = 0.10).

**Step 1 — Water from hydrogen combustion:**
$$m_{H_2O,\,H} = \dfrac{9 \times 5.4}{100} = 0.486\,\text{kg H_2O / kg fuel}$$

**Step 2 — Total water (hydrogen-derived + moisture):**
$$m_{H_2O,\,total} = 0.486 + 0.10 = 0.586\,\text{kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Latent heat lost:**
$$Q_{lost} = 0.586 \times 2.442 = 1.4310\,\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — LHV:**
$$LHV = 16.70 - 1.43 = 15.27\,\text{MJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{LHV \approx 15.27\,\text{MJ/kg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb:** $LHV \approx HHV - 0.22 \times H\% - 2.44 \times M_w$
> This is a **quick-exam shortcut** examiners accept if Dulong's is shown first.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Annual Energy Yield from a Wheat Field

**Scenario:** A farm has 1 hectare of wheat producing **5 tonnes of straw per hectare per harvest** (in addition to grain). If combusted at **80 % boiler efficiency**, calculate the **annual thermal energy** and **electricity** (assuming 30 % generator efficiency).

**Step 1 — Total straw mass:**
$$m = 5\,\text{tonnes} = 5000\,\text{kg per harvest}$$

**Step 2 — Use HHV = 16.70 MJ/kg (from Section 3.1):**
$$E_{thermal,\,gross} = 5000 \times 16.70 = 83{,}500\,\text{MJ}$$

**Step 3 — Apply boiler efficiency $\eta_{b} = 0.80$:**
$$E_{thermal,\,useful} = 83{,}500 \times 0.80 = 66{,}800\,\text{MJ} = 18.56\,\text{MWh}$$

**Step 4 — Electricity via generator ($\eta_{g} = 0.30$):**
$$E_{electric} = 18.56 \times 0.30 = 5.57\,\text{MWh}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kWh (1 MWh = 1000 kWh):**
$$\boxed{E_{electric} \approx 5570\,\text{kWh per hectare per harvest}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Average Indian rural household consumption:** ~1000 kWh/year.
> Therefore, **1 hectare of wheat straw ≈ 5.5 rural homes electrified for one year** — a powerful KTU exam closing line.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — CO₂ Avoidance Calculation (Carbon Credit Logic)

**Step 1 — Carbon in the straw (41.5 %):**
$$m_C = 5000 \times 0.415 = 2075\,\text{kg C}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to CO₂ (molar mass: C = 12, CO₂ = 44):**
$$m_{CO_2} = 2075 \times \dfrac{44}{12} = 7608.3\,\text{kg CO}_2$$

**Step 3 — Subtract fossil-fuel equivalent (assume coal with 25 % efficiency that would have produced the same heat):**
The fossil fuel counterfactual is roughly **2.0–2.2 kg CO₂ per kg of straw equivalent** in open burning. A net avoided emission factor is **~1.5 kg CO₂/kg straw** (conservative LCA value).

**Step 4 — Net avoided CO₂:**
$$\boxed{CO_{2,\,avoided} = 5000 \times 1.5 = 7500\,\text{kg CO}_2 = 7.5\,\text{tonnes per hectare per harvest}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **CDM (Clean Development Mechanism)**-style calculation that KTU examiners love under the **Sustainable Development Goal 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy)** and **SDG 13 (Climate Action)** themes.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Critical Property Comparison Across Straw Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is **the single most important comparison** for KTU 7-mark "compare and discuss" questions.

| Property | Rice Straw | Wheat Straw | Barley Straw | Maize Stalk | Ragi Straw |
|----------|-----------|-------------|--------------|-------------|------------|
| C (%) | 36 – 41 | 41 – 45 | 42 – 46 | 40 – 44 | 38 – 42 |
| H (%) | 4.8 – 5.4 | 5.2 – 5.8 | 5.3 – 5.9 | 5.0 – 5.6 | 4.9 – 5.5 |
| O (%) | 38 – 43 | 38 – 42 | 38 – 42 | 38 – 42 | 40 – 44 |
| Ash (%) | 14 – 20 | 5 – 9 | 5 – 8 | 6 – 10 | 8 – 12 |
| K₂O in Ash (%) | 10 – 20 | 12 – 22 | 10 – 18 | 8 – 15 | 8 – 14 |
| Cl (%) | 0.4 – 0.8 | 0.1 – 0.4 | 0.1 – 0.3 | 0.2 – 0.5 | 0.1 – 0.3 |

| Slagging Risk | **Very High** | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate-High | Moderate |
| Best End Use | Pellets (after washing) | Direct combustion, pellets | Combustion | Combustion | Briquettes |

# 🏗️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Straw-to-Energy Conversion Pathway (Block Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **complete functional flow** from field residue to end-use energy vectors, capturing all major conversion routes covered in Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Field: Cereal Crops] --> B[Grain Harvest]
    B --> C[Straw Residue Generation]
    C --> D[Collection: Baling or Loose]

    D --> E{Pre-Processing}
    E --> E1[Field Drying: M lt 20%]
    E --> E2[Shredding: lt 50 mm]
    E --> E3[Size Reduction: lt 10 mm]

    E1 --> F{Conversion Pathway}
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F

    F --> F1[Direct Combustion 800 to 1200 C]
    F --> F2[Pyrolysis 300 to 700 C no O2]
    F --> F3[Gasification 700 to 900 C limited O2]
    F --> F4[Densification: Pellets and Briquettes]

    F1 --> G1[Hot Water / Steam]
    F1 --> G2[Electricity via Turbine]
    F2 --> G3[Bio-oil and Bio-char]
    F3 --> G4[Producer Gas CO and H2]
    F4 --> F1
    F4 --> F3
    F4 --> F5[Domestic Heating Fuel]

    G1 --> H1[District Heating]
    G2 --> H2[Grid Electricity]
    G3 --> H3[Chemical Industry]
    G4 --> H4[IC Engine and CHP]
    F5 --> H5[Rural Household Cooking]

    H1 --> I[End User]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow proceeds **vertically downward** from field generation to end-user. The **pre-processing branch (E)** is the **most critical step** in any KTU answer — examiners award 1 mark for explicitly mentioning drying and size reduction.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Combustion Process of a Straw Particle (Reaction Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Moist Straw lt 50 C] --> B[Drying Stage: 100 to 150 C]
    B --> C[Devolatilization Stage: 200 to 500 C]
    C --> D[Volatile Combustion: 500 to 900 C]
    D --> E[Char Combustion: gt 700 C]
    E --> F[Ash Residue: gt 800 C]

    C --> G[Light Hydrocarbons]
    C --> H[CO and CO2 and H2O]
    C --> I[Tars and Heavy HCs]
    D --> J[Flame Region: Yellow Luminous]
    E --> K[Char Particles: Red Glow]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage-wise duration (for a 5 mm straw particle):**
> - Drying: ~30 s
> - Devolatilization: ~60 s
> - Char combustion: ~120 s
> The **char stage dominates** total burn time, which is why dense chars (high lignin) give longer burn.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Densification Process Flow (Pellet Manufacturing)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Loose Straw Bales] --> B[Hammer Mill: Grind to 3 to 5 mm]
    B --> C[Dryer: Reduce Moisture to 8 to 12%]
    C --> D[Conditioner: Steam at 80 to 110 C]
    D --> E[Pellet Mill: Roller and Die at 80 to 150 MPa]
    E --> F[Cooler: Drop to Ambient]
    F --> G[Sieve: Remove Fines]
    G --> H[Packed Straw Pellets: 6 to 8 mm dia]
    H --> I[Storage Silo: Bulk or Bagged]
    I --> J[End User: Boiler or CHP]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note on Lignin:** During steam conditioning, lignin softens (Tg ≈ 70–90 °C) and acts as a **natural binder** — this is why pellets hold shape without added glue.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Slagging Mechanism in Straw Combustion (Cause-and-Effect Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Straw Combustion in Furnace] --> B[Ash Formation: SiO2 and K2O and Cl]
    B --> C[Gas-Phase Alkali Release: KCl and NaCl]
    C --> D[Condensation on Heat Exchanger Tubes]
    D --> E[Sticky Layer Formation: 600 to 800 C]
    E --> F[Capture of Fly Ash Particles]
    F --> G[Build-up of Slag Layer]
    G --> H[Reduced Heat Transfer: Tube Failure]
    H --> I[Plant Shutdown: Loss of Generation]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Remediation Path (to mention in KTU answers):** Additive injection of **kaolin (Al₂O₃·2SiO₂·2H₂O)** at 1–2 % of fuel mass captures K as **KAlSi₃O₈ (leucite)**, which melts at >1300 °C → does NOT slag. Add this to any slagging discussion for **extra credit**.

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Set-A]`
**Define "Straw" as a fuel. List any four engineering challenges encountered when using loose straw as a fuel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Straw is the **dry, lignocellulosic stalk residue** of cereal crops such as rice, wheat, barley, maize, and oats, left in the field after grain harvesting. It is a **secondary agricultural biomass fuel** with HHV in the range **14–17 MJ/kg** (dry basis).

**Four Engineering Challenges (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Low bulk density (60–120 kg/m³):** Inflates transport and storage cost; demands densification.
2. **High ash content (10–20 %):** Especially in rice straw; causes fouling and slagging.
3. **High alkali (K, Na) and Chlorine content:** Forms low-melting KCl → corrodes boiler tubes.
4. **High moisture at harvest (20–60 %):** Reduces combustion efficiency; requires pre-drying.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Set-B]`
**Differentiate between Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV) of a biomass fuel. Why is LHV more relevant for boiler design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**HHV Definition (1 mark):** HHV is the total heat released by **complete combustion of unit mass of fuel**, with all products of combustion (including water) returning to **liquid state at 25 °C** — i.e., the latent heat of water condensation is recovered.

**LHV Definition (1 mark):** LHV is the heat released when the **water formed remains in vapour state**, and the latent heat of vaporization is **NOT recovered**. Numerically, LHV < HHV.

**Relevance to Boilers (1 mark):** In most practical boiler and gasification systems, the **flue gas leaves at temperatures well above 100 °C**, so water **escapes as steam**. The latent heat of condensation is therefore **lost to the flue gas**, making **LHV the thermodynamically appropriate value** for efficiency calculations.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]`
**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **four thermochemical conversion routes** of straw into useful energy. Compare their **operating temperatures** and **end products**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rice straw sample has the following composition: **C = 38 %, H = 5.2 %, O = 41 %, N = 0.8 %, S = 0.1 %, Ash = 14.9 %**. The moisture content at the time of use is **12 %**. Calculate **(i)** the HHV using Dulong's formula, **(ii)** the LHV at 12 % moisture, and **(iii)** the **fuel ratio** if the volatile matter is 75 % and fixed carbon is 13 %. Comment on the reactivity of the fuel. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Straw can be converted into useful energy through **four thermochemical routes** depending on the **oxygen supply and operating temperature**: direct combustion, pyrolysis, gasification, and densification-assisted combustion/co-firing.

**Conversion Routes Table (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Route | O₂ Availability | Temperature (°C) | Main Products | Best Use |
|-------|------------------|------------------|----------------|----------|
| **Direct Combustion** | Excess (λ > 1) | 800 – 1200 | Heat, steam, ash | Boilers, CHP |
| **Pyrolysis** | None (inert N₂) | 300 – 700 | Bio-oil, bio-char, non-condensable gases | Specialty chemicals, soil amendment |
| **Gasification** | Sub-stoichiometric (0.2 < λ < 0.4) | 700 – 900 | Producer gas (CO, H₂, CH₄, N₂) | IC engines, FT synthesis |
| **Densification + Co-firing** | Co-firing with coal | 800 – 1100 | Mixed steam/electricity | Retrofit of existing coal plants |

**Flowchart (2 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.1 for the complete Mermaid flow; student should sketch the four routes branching from "Straw Pre-Processing".)*

**Conclusion (0 marks — not counted in 7, but adds polish):**
Co-firing with coal is the most commercially established route globally.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- C = 38 %, H = 5.2 %, O = 41 %, N = 0.8 %, S = 0.1 %, Ash = 14.9 %
- Moisture (M_w) = 12 % = 0.12 kg/kg wet fuel
- VM = 75 %, FC = 13 % (note: VM + FC + Ash + Moisture on as-received basis; for fuel ratio we use dry basis)

**Step 1 (i) — Dulong's HHV Calculation (3 marks):**

$$HHV = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,A$$

Substituting:

$$HHV = (0.3491 \times 38) + (1.1783 \times 5.2) + (0.1005 \times 0.1) - (0.1034 \times 41) - (0.0151 \times 0.8) - (0.0211 \times 14.9)$$

$$HHV = 13.2658 + 6.1272 + 0.0101 - 4.2394 - 0.0121 - 0.3144$$

$$HHV = 14.8372\,\text{MJ/kg (dry basis)}$$

$$\boxed{HHV \approx 14.84\,\text{MJ/kg (dry)}}$$

**Step 2 (ii) — LHV at 12 % moisture (2 marks):**

$$LHV = HHV - 2.442 \times \left( \frac{9H}{100} + M_w \right)$$

$$LHV = 14.84 - 2.442 \times \left( \frac{9 \times 5.2}{100} + 0.12 \right)$$

$$LHV = 14.84 - 2.442 \times (0.468 + 0.12) = 14.84 - 2.442 \times 0.588$$

$$LHV = 14.84 - 1.4359 = 13.40\,\text{MJ/kg (as received basis)}$$

$$\boxed{LHV \approx 13.40\,\text{MJ/kg}}$$

**Step 3 (iii) — Fuel Ratio & Reactivity Comment (2 marks):**

$$\text{Fuel Ratio} = \frac{FC}{VM} = \frac{13}{75} = 0.173$$

$$\boxed{\text{Fuel Ratio} = 0.173 \quad (\ll 1)}$$

**Comment (1 mark within Step 3):**
Since the fuel ratio is **far less than 2**, the straw is a **highly reactive fuel**, meaning it:
- Ignites very easily.
- Burns with a **long, smoky, volatile-rich flame**.
- Produces **little char** (because FC is low).
- Is **unsuitable for use in domestic stoves** without proper design.
- Requires **carefully designed combustion chambers** (e.g., suspension burners, fluidized beds).

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`
**(a)** Discuss the **composition of straw** (ultimate and proximate) and explain why its **Calorific Value is lower than that of coal** despite having comparable carbon content. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wheat farm produces **8 tonnes of straw per hectare**. The straw is converted to **producer gas** in a downdraft gasifier with a **cold gas efficiency of 70 %** and used in a **dual-fuel engine** (80 % diesel + 20 % diesel) generating electricity at **30 % electrical efficiency**. Calculate: **(i)** Thermal energy content of the straw, **(ii)** Chemical energy in the producer gas, **(iii)** Electrical energy generated, and **(iv)** Number of rural households (annual consumption 1200 kWh) that can be served. **(Use HHV = 16.7 MJ/kg.)* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Ultimate Composition (2 marks):**

| Element | % in Straw | % in Coal (Bituminous) |
|---------|-----------|------------------------|
| C | 35 – 45 | 65 – 80 |
| H | 5 – 6 | 4 – 6 |
| O | 35 – 45 | 5 – 15 |
| N | 0.3 – 1.5 | 1 – 2 |
| S | 0.05 – 0.2 | 0.5 – 2.5 |
| Cl | 0.1 – 0.8 | < 0.05 |

**Proximate Composition (2 marks):**

| Component | % in Straw | % in Coal |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Moisture | 8 – 15 | 5 – 15 |
| Volatile Matter | 70 – 85 | 25 – 35 |
| Fixed Carbon | 12 – 20 | 45 – 60 |
| Ash | 5 – 20 | 8 – 15 |

**Why HHV of Straw < Coal (3 marks):**

1. **High Oxygen Content:** Straw has 35–45 % O (vs. 5–15 % in coal). The H atoms in straw are already partially bound to O as –OH groups, so they **release less energy upon combustion**. Each 1 % of "extra" O reduces HHV by ~0.10 MJ/kg.

2. **Lower Fixed Carbon:** Coal has FC = 45–60 %, while straw has FC = 12–20 %. Char combustion contributes a major share of the heat in coal.

3. **High Ash Content (especially in rice straw):** Ash carries no energy and absorbs some heat for its own heating.

4. **Lower Energy Density of C in Lignocellulose:** The C in straw is partly "diluted" in the polysaccharide structure, while the C in coal is in dense aromatic rings.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** m = 8 tonnes = 8000 kg, HHV = 16.7 MJ/kg, η_gasifier = 70 %, η_engine = 30 %.

**Step 1 (i) — Thermal Energy Content of Straw (1.5 marks):**
$$E_{straw} = m \times HHV = 8000 \times 16.7 = 133{,}600\,\text{MJ}$$

**Step 2 (ii) — Chemical Energy in Producer Gas (1.5 marks):**
$$E_{gas} = E_{straw} \times \eta_{gasifier} = 133{,}600 \times 0.70 = 93{,}520\,\text{MJ}$$

**Step 3 (iii) — Electrical Energy Generated (2 marks):**
$$E_{electric} = E_{gas} \times \eta_{engine} = 93{,}520 \times 0.30 = 28{,}056\,\text{MJ}$$

Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):

$$E_{electric} = \dfrac{28{,}056}{3.6} = 7793.3\,\text{kWh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{electric} \approx 7793\,\text{kWh per hectare per harvest}}$$

**Step 4 (iv) — Number of Rural Households (2 marks):**
$$N = \dfrac{E_{electric}}{\text{Consumption per household}} = \dfrac{7793}{1200} = 6.49$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 6 \text{ rural households per hectare per harvest}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Straw HHV Problems:**
> 1. **Unit trap:** HHV must be in **MJ/kg**; mass in **kg**; energy comes out in **MJ**. Students commonly convert to kWh at the end — that is correct, but only AFTER computing MJ.
> 2. **Dulong's formula requires DRY basis:** If moisture is given, EITHER (a) compute HHV on dry basis and correct for moisture separately, OR (b) reduce C, H, O percentages to dry basis first. Mixing wet and dry numbers is the most common deduction reason.
> 3. **Fuel Ratio comment is COMPULSORY** — failing to comment on reactivity forfeits 1 mark.
> 4. **Straw ≠ Wood:** A common error is to quote wood HHV (17–19 MJ/kg) for straw. Straw is **always 1–2 MJ/kg lower** due to higher ash and higher oxygen content.
> 5. **Renewable ≠ Carbon-zero:** Although straw is renewable, its combustion still releases fossil-fuel-grade CO₂. The "carbon neutral" label applies only on a **life-cycle basis** (with photosynthesis re-fixation). Examiners penalize sloppy terminology.

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU 2024 revision — read this twice on the morning of the exam.**

### 🔑 Core Definitions (memorize verbatim)
- ✅ **Straw:** Lignocellulosic stalk residue of cereal crops, HHV 14–17 MJ/kg (dry).
- ✅ **HHV:** Heat released with water condensed to liquid.
- ✅ **LHV:** Heat released with water remaining as vapour — **use this for boilers**.
- ✅ **Dulong's Formula:** $HHV = 0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211A$.
- ✅ **Fuel Ratio (FC/VM):** < 2 = reactive; > 4 = unreactive. **Straw is highly reactive.**
- ✅ **Bulk Density:** Straw loose = 60–120 kg/m³; pellets = 600–750 kg/m³.

### ⚙️ Engineering Challenges (always mention 4 in long answers)
- ✅ Low bulk density → solve by **densification** (pellets/briquettes at 80–150 MPa).
- ✅ High ash (10–20 %) → solve by **co-firing** or **additives** (kaolin).
- ✅ High alkali + Cl → causes **slagging**; prevent by **pre-washing** or **additives**.
- ✅ High moisture at harvest → must dry to **< 15 %** for combustion.

### 🔥 Conversion Routes (4 — know temperatures)
- ✅ Combustion: 800–1200 °C
- ✅ Pyrolysis: 300–700 °C (no O₂)
- ✅ Gasification: 700–900 °C (limited O₂)
- ✅ Co-firing: 10–20 % straw with coal in existing plants

### 📐 Numerical Must-Knows
- ✅ Dulong's formula constants — memorize the 6 coefficients.
- ✅ $LHV = HHV - 2.442(9H/100 + M_w)$.
- ✅ Carbon-to-CO₂ conversion factor: **× 44/12 = 3.67**.
- ✅ 1 MWh = 3.6 GJ = 3,600 MJ = 1,000 kWh.
- ✅ 1 hectare wheat straw ≈ **5–6 rural households** (annual electrical equivalent).

### 🌍 Sustainability Hooks (for high marks)
- ✅ **SDG 7** (Affordable & Clean Energy) — straw-based CHP for villages.
- ✅ **SDG 13** (Climate Action) — avoided CO₂ via straw instead of coal.
- ✅ **India's SATAT Scheme** (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation) — uses agricultural residues for compressed biogas.
- ✅ **Punjab/Haryana stubble burning problem** — direct link to *why* straw-to-energy is policy-critical.

### 🚫 Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing straw with hay (hay is dried grass for fodder; straw is the by-product).
- ❌ Quoting HHV on wet basis — always convert to **dry basis** for comparison.
- ❌ Ignoring the LHV correction when moisture is given.
- ❌ Writing "straw is a good fuel" without mentioning its **engineering challenges**.
